figures.name: Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab
figures.title: Founder and First Emir
figures.roleDescription: Founded the Aghlabid dynasty and established semi-autonomous rule in Ifriqiya

figures.name: Ziyadat Allah I
figures.title: Third Aghlabid Emir
figures.roleDescription: Ordered the conquest of Sicily and oversaw major territorial expansion

figures.name: Abu Muhammad Ziyadat Allah I ibn Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab
figures.title: Aghlabid Amir of Ifriqiya
figures.roleDescription: Third Aghlabid ruler who initiated the conquest of Sicily
figures.significance: Launched the Muslim conquest of Sicily and established more stable Aghlabid control over Ifriqiya through alliance with Berbers.
figures.biography:
Ziyadat Allah I was the son of Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab, the founder of the Aghlabid dynasty. He ruled as amir of Ifriqiya from 817 until his death in 838, and his reign marked a shift towards greater control and stability for the emirs in Ifriqiya. Despite being an independent ruler, Ziyadat Allah faced the largest jund revolt in Ifriqiya's history, which broke out in 824 and saw most of the region conquered by the rebelling Arab military forces. Unlike previous rulers who had to deal with jund and Berber problems simultaneously, Ziyadat Allah had established a working relationship with the Berbers.

With this Berber alliance, he was able to employ Berber forces to defeat the rebelling jund, which allowed him not only to avoid losing control but to turn Aghlabid energies towards conquest. In 831, Ziyadat Allah launched an invasion of Sicily, placing the qadi Asad ibn al-Furat in command and terming the military adventure a jihad. This expedition proved successful, with Palermo being made the capital of the region captured. The conquest of Sicily marked a new era for the Aghlabids and is why Ziyadat is sometimes described as the second founder of the Aghlabid regime, having solved the jund and Berber issues that had faced all preceding emirs.
achievements: Launched the Muslim conquest of Sicily
Defeated the largest jund revolt in Ifriqiya
Established stable alliance with Berber tribes
Built ribats at Monastir and Susa

figures.name: Ibrahim II ibn Ahmad
figures.title: Emir of Ifriqiya
figures.roleDescription: Founded the palace-city of Raqqada and personally led military campaigns in Sicily

figures.name: Abu Ishaq Ibrahim II ibn Ahmad
figures.title: Emir of Ifriqiya
figures.roleDescription: Aghlabid ruler known for his military campaigns and architectural projects
figures.significance: Completed the conquest of Sicily and built the palace-city of Raqqada, but gained a reputation for cruelty and tyranny.
figures.biography:
Ibrahim II was the son of the Aghlabid emir Ahmad of Ifriqiya and ruled from 875 until his abdication in 902. During his brother Muhammad II's frivolous reign, Ibrahim served as governor of Kairouan with exemplary efficiency, earning him much admiration. When Muhammad II died in 875, Ibrahim's candidacy was supported by popular crowds and endorsed by the jurists of Kairouan. Among his major public works, Ibrahim completed construction of the Zaytuna mosque of Tunis, enlarged the Uqba mosque of Kairouan, built a vast new water reservoir for the city, erected the walls of Sousse, and established naval signal towers along the Ifriqiyan coast. Most notably, in 876 he erected the new palace-city of Raqqada just southwest of Kairouan, built on a grandiose scale with walls ten kilometers long.

As his reign progressed, Ibrahim acquired a reputation for tyranny and homicidal sadism, with chronicles reporting that he took great pleasure in cruelty and killing, often conducting executions personally. He was a centralizing ruler who mistrusted the old Arab aristocracy and sought to undermine the semi-autonomous Arab regiments by expanding his Sudanese slave-soldier regiments to as much as 10,000 men. Under Ibrahim's rule, most of Sicily was completed in Muslim hands - Syracuse fell in 878, and after his final campaign, Taormina fell in 902, completing the 75-year conquest. However, mounting reports of his cruel atrocities prompted the Abbasid Caliph al-Mu'tadid to recall him to Baghdad in 901-902, leading to his abdication and final campaign where he died of dysentery near Cosenza in 902.
achievements: Completed the conquest of Sicily
Built the palace-city of Raqqada
Completed major mosque and infrastructure projects
Established extensive naval signal tower system

figures.name: Ziyadat Allah III
figures.title: Last Aghlabid Emir
figures.roleDescription: Final ruler of the Aghlabid dynasty, overthrown by the Fatimids
figures.significance: Last ruler of the Aghlabid dynasty, whose weakness led to the Fatimid conquest of Ifriqiya in 909.
figures.biography:
Ziyadat Allah III was the eleventh and final Aghlabid amir, ruling from 902-909. He came to power after his father Ibrahim II's death during the siege of Cosenza in 902. Due to insecurity stemming from his father's assassination and the dynasty's internal conflicts, Ziyadat Allah III ordered the execution of his rival brothers and uncles, which significantly weakened the dynasty during a critical period when the Fatimids were mounting their assault against Aghlabid domains.

His reign coincided with the rise of the Fatimid movement in the far west of Ifriqiya, led by Abu Abdallah al-Shi'i among the Kutama Berbers. The Fatimids launched frequent attacks that provoked general unrest and increased Aghlabid political instability. The weakened state, crippled by Ibrahim II's destruction of the Arab aristocracy and the departure of much of the Ifriqiyan army for the Italian campaign, proved unable to resist the Kutama advance. The Fatimids eventually managed to capture Kairouan in 909, forcing Ziyadat Allah III to evacuate his palace at Raqqada and flee, marking the end of the Aghlabid dynasty after over a century of rule.

figures.name: Abu Abdallah al-Shi'i
figures.title: Fatimid Da'i and Military Leader
figures.roleDescription: Ismaili missionary who conquered the Aghlabid state for the Fatimids
figures.significance: Led the Kutama Berber forces that conquered Aghlabid Ifriqiya and established Fatimid rule in 909.
figures.biography:
Abu Abdallah al-Shi'i was an Ismaili da'i (missionary) originally from San'a in Yemen who came to the Maghreb to propagate Ismaili beliefs. In 893, he first settled among the Banu Saktan tribe, part of the larger Kutama tribe, in Ikjan near the city of Mila in northeastern Algeria. Due to hostility from local Aghlabid authorities and other Kutama tribes, he was forced to seek protection from the Banu Ghashman tribe in Tazrut, from where he began building support for the Fatimid movement.

Abu Abdallah established an Isma'ili theocratic state based in Tazrut and gradually won over most Kutama tribes through persuasion or coercion. Over the following years, he led increasingly successful military campaigns against the Aghlabids, capturing key cities including Mila, Setif, Tubna, and Baghaya. On 25 February 909, he set out from Ikjan with an army of 200,000 men for the final invasion of Kairouan. After defeating the last Aghlabid forces near al-Aribus on 18 March, he entered Raqqada on 25 March 909, effectively ending Aghlabid rule. He then led his army west to retrieve Abdallah al-Mahdi from Sijilmasa, who was proclaimed the first Fatimid caliph in 910, though Abu Abdallah was later killed in a dispute with his former protégé.
achievements: Conquered Aghlabid Ifriqiya for the Fatimids
Organized and led Kutama Berber forces
Established Ismaili theocratic state
Ended over a century of Aghlabid rule

figures.name: Wadha bint Muhammad Al Orair
figures.title: Khalidi Princess
figures.roleDescription: Mother of Saudi royalty
figures.significance: She was a Khalidi princess who became the mother of Prince Turki and King Saud of Saudi Arabia.
figures.biography:
Wadha bint Muhammad Al Orair was a princess from the Al 'Ura'yir family, which was the ruling clan of the Bani Khalid tribe that controlled eastern Arabia. The Al 'Ura'yir were part of the Al Humaid branch that traditionally held chieftainship of the Bani Khalid confederation.

She became historically significant through her marriage into the Saudi royal family, becoming the mother of both Prince Turki and King Saud. This connection represents an important matrimonial alliance between the Bani Khalid rulers and the emerging Saudi state, despite the two powers being political rivals during much of the 18th and early 19th centuries. Her lineage connected the ancient ruling families of eastern Arabia with the founders of modern Saudi Arabia.
achievements: Mother of King Saud of Saudi Arabia
Mother of Prince Turki

figures.name: Sheikh Abdullah Abu Risha
figures.title: Paramount Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Traditional tribal leader and modern political figure

figures.name: Ahmed Abu Risha
figures.title: Sheikh and Tribal Leader
figures.roleDescription: Leader of the Anbar Salvation Council and political coalition leader
figures.significance: Led the Anbar Awakening movement against al-Qaeda and became a prominent Sunni political leader in Iraq.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Ahmed Bezaa Abu Risha was born in 1969 as a member of the Dulaim tribe and is three years older than his brother Abdul Sattar. Following his brother Abdul Sattar's assassination by a roadside bomb in September 2007, Ahmed was selected by the sheiks of Anbar province to take over leadership of the Anbar Salvation Council. He met with President Barack Obama both before and after the 2008 Presidential Election and established a newspaper entitled Sahwat al-Iraq (Iraq's Awakening).

In politics, Abu Risha's Iraq Awakening and Independents National Alliance was the most successful coalition in the 2009 Anbar governorate election, winning 8 of 29 seats. However, the Alliance fared poorly in the 2010 parliamentary election, winning only 2.66% of the vote and 4 seats, with many votes going to Ayad Allawi's Iraqi National Movement. Abu Risha has been critical of Iranian interference in Iraq and supports secular and nationalistic parties over religious ones.
achievements: Led the Anbar Awakening movement after his brother's death
Most successful political coalition in 2009 Anbar governorate election
Met with President Barack Obama multiple times
Established Iraq's Awakening newspaper

figures.name: Omar Abu Risha
figures.title: Poet and Diplomat
figures.roleDescription: Syrian poet who wrote the national anthem and served as ambassador to multiple countries

figures.name: Abdul Sattar Abu Risha
figures.title: Sheikh and Tribal Leader
figures.roleDescription: Leader of the Sahawat al-Anbar (Anbar Awakening) alliance against al-Qaeda
figures.significance: Founded the Anbar Salvation Council that opposed al-Qaeda in Iraq and significantly reduced violence in Anbar province.
figures.biography:
Abdul Sattar Abu Risha was the grandson of a tribal leader in the 1920 Iraqi Revolt and son of an officer who served in the Anglo-Iraqi War in 1941. He was the leader of the Abu-Risha tribe and reportedly ran a construction and import-export business with offices in Amman, Jordan and Dubai. During the Iraq insurgency, as al-Qaeda fighters tightened their grip on Ramadi and became increasingly repressive, they killed Abdul Sattar's father and two brothers. In late summer 2006, he began enlisting fellow sheiks in Sahawat al-Anbar and encouraging tribe members to join the local police force.

With U.S. forces providing security for initial meetings at his compound in western Ramadi, these gatherings grew into the Anbar Salvation Council by fall 2006, eventually counting 41 clans from Anbar province by March 2007. The development led to a sharp reduction of violence in the province and forced many al-Qaeda fighters to flee to other regions of Iraq. Abdul Sattar was assassinated on September 13, 2007, along with three guards by an improvised explosive device near his house in Ramadi. The Islamic State of Iraq claimed responsibility for the attack.
achievements: Founded the Sahawat al-Anbar (Anbar Awakening)
Formed alliance of 41 clans against al-Qaeda
Significantly reduced violence in Anbar province
Led successful tribal opposition to al-Qaeda in Iraq

figures.name: Zulaikha Abu Risha
figures.title: Poet and Women's Rights Activist
figures.roleDescription: Prominent Jordanian poet, writer, and advocate for gender-inclusive Arabic language
figures.significance: A leading voice in Arabic feminist literature and advocate for making the Arabic language more gender-inclusive.
figures.biography:
Zulaikha Abd ar-Rahman Abu Risha was born in 1942 in Acre, describing herself as having Palestinian, Jordanian, and Syrian roots. She studied Arabic literature at the University of Jordan, graduating with a bachelor's degree in 1966 and a master's in 1989, later pursuing a doctorate at the University of Exeter where she wrote her thesis on 'Women in Arabic Feminist Literature'. She has been considered a prominent member of the Jordanian literary scene as both a poet and fiction writer.

In 1987, Abu Risha published the short story collection 'In the Cell,' winning a prize from the University of Jordan. She has written at least 10 books of poetry beginning in 1998, autobiographical essays, children's literature, and academic studies. Her work extends to feminist criticism, literature, art, and gender and language studies. She has served as editor of magazines, director of feminist publishing houses, and as a university lecturer and director of the Center for Women's Studies in Amman. Her advocacy for women's rights since the early 1980s has made her a target of extremist groups and lawsuits from Amman's Public Prosecution Office.
achievements: Published over 10 books of poetry and numerous literary works
Won University of Jordan prize for short story collection 'In the Cell'
Served as judge for International Prize for Arabic Fiction in 2019
Authored books on gender-inclusive Arabic language
Produced 'Timeless Tales: Folktales Told by Syrian Refugees'

figures.name: Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah
figures.title: Founder of Al Sadd Sports Club
figures.roleDescription: Founder and early leader of Qatar's most successful football club
figures.significance: Founded Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969, which became Qatar's most successful football club with 64 official championships.
figures.biography:
Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah was the principal founder of Al Sadd Sports Club, established on 21 October 1969 in Doha, Qatar. He was born and raised in the Al Sadd district of Doha, which gave the club its name. The Al-Attiyah family members excelled in football but did not wish to join any of the existing football clubs at the time.

After consulting with Jassim bin Hamad Al Thani, who was the minister of Youth and Sports, Ali bin Hamad Al-Attiyah and his relatives decided to create their own club. They presented their application and club statute to Abdulaziz Buwazair, the operating manager of the Supreme Sports Committee, resulting in the formation of Al Sadd Sports Club. In the club's initial year, Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah oversaw the club's performance and made crucial decisions while his relative Hamad bin Mubarak Al Attiyah coached the team.

The club's initial popularity was owed in part to its affiliation with the Al Attiyah tribe, described as a prominent local tribe. Under his leadership and vision, Al Sadd went on to become the most successful sports club in Qatar, winning their first official league title in 1973-74 and eventually accumulating a national record of 64 official football championships.
achievements: Founded Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969
Established Qatar's most successful football club
Led club to its first league championship in 1971-72

figures.name: Hamad bin Mubarak Al Attiyah
figures.title: First Coach of Al Sadd Sports Club
figures.roleDescription: First coach and founding member of Al Sadd Sports Club
figures.significance: Served as the first coach of Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969 and was part of the founding Al Attiyah family group.
figures.biography:
Hamad bin Mubarak Al Attiyah was one of the founding members of the Al Attiyah family group that established Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969. He served as the club's first coach during its inaugural year of establishment, when the team trained on a football pitch in a local high school in Doha.

While his relative Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah oversaw the club's overall performance and made crucial decisions as the founder, Hamad bin Mubarak Al Attiyah took on the practical role of coaching the team. This division of responsibilities between the Al Attiyah family members helped establish the organizational structure that would contribute to the club's early success and eventual dominance in Qatari football.
achievements: First coach of Al Sadd Sports Club
Founding member of Al Sadd Sports Club
Helped establish the club's early organizational structure

figures.name: Sheikha Aisha bint Hamad Al Attiyah
figures.title: Wife of Former Emir Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani
figures.roleDescription: Mother of former Emir Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani
figures.significance: Mother of Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, who served as Emir of Qatar from 1995 to 2013, representing the Al Attiyah family's connection to the ruling family.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Aisha bint Hamad Al Attiyah was one of the wives of Sheikh Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani, who served as Emir of Qatar from 1972 to 1995. She was the mother of Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa, who would later become Emir of Qatar from 1995 to 2013 after deposing his father in a bloodless coup.

Her marriage to Sheikh Khalifa represented an important alliance between the Al Attiyah family and the ruling Al Thani family. Through her son Hamad bin Khalifa, the Al Attiyah family maintained significant influence in Qatar's political landscape, as Hamad bin Khalifa became one of Qatar's most transformative rulers, modernizing the country and establishing it as a major regional power.
achievements: Mother of Emir Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani
Connected Al Attiyah family to ruling Al Thani dynasty

figures.name: Sheikha Al Maha bint Muhammad Al Attiyah
figures.title: Wife of Deputy Emir Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani
figures.roleDescription: Engineer and wife of Qatar's Deputy Emir
figures.significance: Married to Qatar's Deputy Emir Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani in 2012, representing continued Al Attiyah family connections to the ruling family.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Al Maha bint Muhammad Al Attiyah is an accomplished engineer who earned her degree from the Qatar University College of Engineering and Computer Science. She is the daughter of Sheikh Muhammad bin Abdullah bin Hamad Al Attiyah, who served as Premier and Chairman of the Administrative Control and Transparency Authority.

On 22 March 2012, she married Sheikh Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani at Al Wajbah Palace. Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani is the Deputy Emir of Qatar and unofficial heir presumptive to the throne, having been appointed to this position by his half-brother Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani in November 2014. Her marriage represents the continuation of strong ties between the Al Attiyah family and Qatar's ruling Al Thani family, maintaining the family's influential position in Qatari society.
achievements: Engineering degree from Qatar University
Married to Deputy Emir of Qatar
Daughter of former Premier of Qatar

figures.name: Nasser Al-Attiyah
figures.title: Rally Driver and Dakar Rally Winner
figures.roleDescription: Professional rally driver and Olympic shooter
figures.significance: Won the 2011 Dakar Rally alongside German co-driver Timo Gottschalk, becoming the first Qatari to win this prestigious motorsport event.
figures.biography:
Nasser Al-Attiyah is a prominent Qatari rally driver who achieved international recognition by winning the 2011 Dakar Rally. He partnered with German co-driver Timo Gottschalk for this historic victory, representing a significant achievement for Qatari motorsport.

Al-Attiyah first competed in the Dakar Rally in 2010 alongside Gottschalk, where they won four stages and finished as runners-up to Volkswagen teammates Carlos Sainz and Lucas Cruz, losing by just over two minutes. The following year in 2011, Al-Attiyah and Gottschalk returned with determination and assumed the lead of the rally after victory in the eighth stage. When problems befell their main competitors Sainz and Cruz in the eleventh stage, Al-Attiyah and Gottschalk built a commanding lead of over 50 minutes that they maintained until the end of the rally, securing Qatar's first Dakar Rally victory.
achievements: Winner of 2011 Dakar Rally
Runner-up in 2010 Dakar Rally
Won four stages in 2010 Dakar Rally
First Qatari to win Dakar Rally

figures.name: Haitham bin Tariq Al Said
figures.title: Sultan of Oman
figures.roleDescription: Current Sultan of Oman since 2020
figures.significance: Became Sultan of Oman in 2020 following Qaboos bin Said's death, chosen as successor according to Qaboos's sealed letter.
figures.biography:
Haitham bin Tariq Al Said is the cousin of the late Sultan Qaboos and became the current Sultan of Oman on 11 January 2020. He was previously Minister of Heritage and National Culture and was considered one of the top three contenders to succeed Qaboos, along with his brothers Asa'ad bin Tariq and Shihab bin Tariq. He is the son of Sayyid Tariq bin Taimur, who was Oman's first prime minister and Qaboos's former father-in-law.

When Qaboos died without naming a public heir, the Royal Family Council opened the sealed letter containing Qaboos's choice for successor, which named Haitham bin Tariq as the intended heir. The royal court quickly announced their decision to defer to Qaboos's written choice. In 2021, Sultan Haitham created the position of Crown Prince of Oman by Royal Decree, appointing his eldest son Theyazin bin Haitham to the role, ensuring succession planning for the dynasty.
achievements: Became Sultan of Oman following constitutional succession process
Created the position of Crown Prince in 2021
Appointed his son as first Crown Prince of Oman

figures.name: Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi
figures.title: Imam of Oman
figures.roleDescription: Founder of the Al Bu Said dynasty
figures.significance: Founded the Al Bu Said dynasty in 1744 and liberated Oman from Persian occupation.
figures.biography:
Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi was born in Adam, Oman in 1694, the son of Sa'id bin Ahmad Al-Busaidi. He came from the Al Bu Sa'id, a small Hinawi tribe from the interior of Oman and was great-great-grandson of Mubarak al-Saidi al-Azdy of the Banu Hiba. Beginning as a wood vendor, he worked his way up to become a leading merchant of the port city of Sohar and was appointed governor of this city in 1737.

When Persian forces under Nader Shah occupied much of Oman between 1742-1744, Ahmad defended Sohar during a nine-month siege and eventually negotiated terms with the Persian commander. By 1747, he had invited the remaining Persian troops to a banquet at his fort in Barka where he massacred them, effectively ending Persian occupation. After defeating rival claimant Bal'arab bin Himyar in 1749, the Ibadi tribes of Oman elected Ahmad bin Said as their Imam on 9 June 1749 at Rustaq.

Ahmad ruled for 39 generally peaceful years from his seat at Rustaq, encouraging the development of agriculture and maritime trading while maintaining Oman's first permanent army and navy. He reestablished Oman's leading position among the Persian Gulf states and attempted to gain control of the strategic Strait of Hormuz in the late 1770s. He died on 15 December 1783 at Al-Batinah Fort, Rustaq, where he is buried near the Great Mosque.
achievements: Founded the Al Bu Said dynasty in 1744
Liberated Oman from Persian occupation
Established Oman's first permanent army and navy
Reestablished Oman as a leading Persian Gulf power

figures.name: Said bin Taimur
figures.title: Sultan of Muscat and Oman
figures.roleDescription: 13th Sultan, father of Qaboos, overthrown in 1970 coup
figures.significance: His conservative policies led to his overthrow by his son Qaboos in 1970, ending his isolationist rule.
figures.biography:
Said bin Taimur was born on 13 August 1910 and became the 13th Sultan of Muscat and Oman on 10 February 1932 at age 21, succeeding his father Taimur bin Feisal who had abdicated for financial reasons. He inherited an administration heavily in debt to Britain. He attended Mayo College in India from 1922-1927 and later studied in Baghdad. He secured British recognition of Oman's independence in 1951 and sold Gwadar to Pakistan for $1 million in 1958.

Despite oil wealth that could have modernized the country, Said became increasingly reclusive and implemented restrictive policies. By 1965, it was forbidden to smoke in public, play football, wear sunglasses, or speak to anyone for more than 15 minutes. The Dhofar province revolted in 1965 with Chinese and Arab nationalist support, followed by an assassination attempt in 1966 that made him even more paranoid. He kept his own son Qaboos under virtual house arrest and refused to speak to him for the last 14 months before the coup.

Under his rule, Oman had an under-5 mortality rate of 25%, widespread disease and malnutrition, only three schools, 5% literacy rate, and just 10 kilometers of paved roads. On 23 July 1970, Qaboos executed a successful British-supported coup against his father at the palace in Salalah. Said was exiled to London where he lived at the Dorchester Hotel until his death on 19 October 1972. He was initially buried at Brookwood Cemetery before his remains were returned to Oman.
achievements: Secured British recognition of Oman's independence in 1951
Sold Gwadar province to Pakistan in 1958
Maintained friendly relations with the United States during WWII

figures.name: Thuwaini bin Said
figures.title: Sultan of Muscat and Oman
figures.roleDescription: Sultan from 1856-1866, ruled Omani portion after empire's division
figures.significance: Became Sultan of Muscat and Oman after his father's death, presiding over the division of the Omani empire between Oman and Zanzibar.
figures.biography:
Thuwaini bin Said al-Busaidi was born in 1821 as the third son of Said bin Sultan. Unlike his brothers, Thuwaini was born in Oman and never visited Zanzibar, serving as his father's representative in Oman when Said was away in Zanzibar. He was appointed heir apparent on 23 July 1844 and had long acted as his father's Governor in Muscat and commander-in-chief of the Saidi forces.

After Said bin Sultan died in 1856, Thuwaini became Sultan of Muscat and Oman while his brother Majid took power in Zanzibar. This led to a struggle between the brothers, with Thuwaini refusing to acknowledge Majid and attempting to regain Zanzibar by force. The British government of India acted as arbitrator, and Lord Canning ruled that the empire should be divided into two separate sultanates: Zanzibar to Majid and Muscat and Oman to Thuwaini, formalized in the 1862 Zanzibar Guarantee Treaty signed in Paris.

Thuwaini insisted that Majid pay 40,000 Maria Theresa thalers annually as compensation for the economic loss caused by the severance of the Zanzibar connection, but the payment fell into arrears and ceased after a year. This left Muscat and Oman in a difficult financial situation, forcing Thuwaini to levy duties on various articles, creating discontent. He was married to his cousin Ralie and had several children. In 1866, he was rumored to have been killed by his own son, Sayyid Salim bin Thuwaini.
achievements: Presided over the formal division of Omani empire in 1862
Served as heir apparent and commander-in-chief under his father
Negotiated compensation payments from Zanzibar for Oman

figures.name: Sultan Saeed Al Darmaki
figures.title: Film Director
figures.roleDescription: Emirati film director
figures.significance: First Emirati director to direct an American fantasy comedy film released internationally.
figures.biography:
Sultan Saeed Al Darmaki is an Emirati film director who made his directorial debut with the 2015 American fantasy comedy film 'Kids vs Monsters.' The film was released on video on demand on September 29, 2015, marking a significant milestone for Emirati cinema in international markets.

The film featured a notable cast including Malcolm McDowell, Lance Henriksen, Keith David, and Elaine Hendrix. The story centered around six children sent to a mysterious house by their wealthy parents, where they face seven monsters intent on battling them. Despite mixed reviews, with some critics calling it 'tedious' while others found it engaging due to its cast and unique approach, Al Darmaki's work represents an important step in Emirati filmmaking reaching global audiences.
achievements: Directed the 2015 film 'Kids vs Monsters'
Made directorial debut with international cast

figures.name: Awad Al Darmaki
figures.title: Writer
figures.roleDescription: Emirati writer and author
figures.significance: Notable Emirati writer mentioned alongside other prominent UAE literary figures.
figures.biography:
Awad Al Darmaki is an Emirati writer who has contributed to the literary landscape of the United Arab Emirates. He is mentioned among notable Emirati authors and writers, indicating his recognition within the country's cultural and literary circles.

Al Darmaki's work is part of the broader movement of Emirati literature that has emerged alongside the development of the UAE as a nation. He represents the Al Darmaki family's contribution to the intellectual and cultural development of the Emirates, moving beyond traditional roles into modern literary expression.

figures.roleDescription: Mother of fourteen children to a former Qatari Foreign Minister
figures.significance: She was the wife of Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad Al Thani, Qatar's Foreign Minister, and mother to nine sons and five daughters.
figures.biography:
Muna bint Jassim Al Hassan Al Dosari was the wife of Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad bin Abdullah bin Jassim bin Muhammed Al Thani, who served as Qatar's Foreign Minister from February 1972 until his death in August 1985. She was the mother of all fourteen of his children - nine sons and five daughters. Her daughters included Muna bint Suhaim, Rudha bint Suhaim, Amna bint Suhaim, Al Anoud bint Suhaim, and Muneera bint Suhaim.

After her husband's sudden death from a heart attack in August 1985, her sons blamed Ghanim Al Kuwari, the minister of information and culture, for not responding promptly to their father's calls for medical attention. The sons were subsequently imprisoned after they attempted to assassinate Al Kuwari, demonstrating the family's significant influence and the political tensions within Qatar's ruling circles at the time.
achievements: Mother to fourteen children of a prominent Qatari political figure

figures.name: Yasser Al-Dosari
figures.title: Imam of Masjid al-Haram
figures.roleDescription: Senior Islamic cleric and Imam at Islam's holiest mosque
figures.significance: He serves as Imam of Masjid al-Haram in Mecca, one of the most prestigious religious positions in Islam.
figures.biography:
Yasser Al-Dosari is a prominent Islamic scholar who serves as Imam of Masjid al-Haram, the Great Mosque of Mecca, which is considered the most significant mosque in Islam and encloses the Kaaba. He was appointed as Imam in 2019 and as Khatib (preacher) in 2022, making him one of the leading religious figures in the Islamic world.

As Imam of Masjid al-Haram, Al-Dosari leads prayers for millions of pilgrims who visit Mecca annually for Hajj and Umrah. The mosque is the main setting for these pilgrimages and serves as the focal point for over 5 million Muslims who perform the Hajj every year. His position places him among the most influential religious leaders in Saudi Arabia and the broader Islamic community, as he delivers sermons and leads prayers at Islam's holiest site.
achievements: Appointed Imam of Masjid al-Haram in 2019
Appointed Khatib of Masjid al-Haram in 2022
Leads prayers for millions of pilgrims annually

figures.name: Sami Jasem Al-Dosari
figures.title: Saudi Arabian footballer
figures.roleDescription: Professional football player
figures.significance: He is a Saudi Arabian footballer born in 1965, representing the Al Dosari family in professional sports.
figures.biography:
Sami Jasem Al-Dosari is a Saudi Arabian footballer who was born in 1965. As a member of the prominent Dawasir tribe, he represents the family's presence in professional sports in Saudi Arabia. His career coincided with the development of football in the Kingdom during the late 20th century.

While specific details about his club career and achievements are not extensively documented in the available research, his inclusion among notable members of the Dawasir tribe indicates his significance within Saudi Arabian football circles. The Dawasir tribe has a strong presence throughout the Gulf region and has produced notable figures across various fields including politics, religion, and sports.

figures.name: Muhammed Reza al-Jaberi
figures.title: Chief of Protocol and Public Relations
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi PMF official
figures.significance: Iraqi Popular Mobilization Forces official who was killed alongside Qasem Soleimani in the 2020 Baghdad airport strike.
figures.biography: Muhammed Reza al-Jaberi served as the chief of protocol and public relations for Iraq's Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF). He was among the ten people killed in the U.S. drone strike on January 3, 2020, at Baghdad International Airport that targeted Iranian General Qasem Soleimani. Al-Jaberi was traveling with Soleimani and Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis, the deputy chairman of the PMF, when their convoy was struck by missiles from an MQ-9 Reaper drone as they departed the airport. The strike was ordered by U.S. President Donald Trump and was intended to prevent what American officials claimed were imminent attacks on U.S. personnel. Al-Jaberi's death was part of a broader escalation in U.S.-Iran tensions that brought the region close to open conflict.

figures.name: Muhammad Saleh Al-Jaberi
figures.title: Scholar and Writer
figures.roleDescription: Tunisian researcher, novelist, and cultural administrator
figures.significance: Prominent Tunisian scholar who specialized in Maghreb literature and served as a cultural administrator with ALECSO.
figures.biography:
Muhammad Saleh Al-Jaberi was born in Tozeur, Tunisia in February 1940 and died in Ariana, Tunisia in June 2009. He specialized in recording the historical and literary movement in the Maghreb, particularly in Algeria, Libya, and Tunisia, making significant contributions to the study of Tunisian literature and culture. Al-Jaberi received his early education in Tozeur, attending a Kuttab and then Ibn-Shubbaṭ elementary school until 1953, before completing his secondary studies at the Secondary Institution in Tozeur. He continued his education at Zaytuna University in Tunis, obtaining a certificate in 1961, and later studied in Baghdad where he obtained a licence. He earned a Master's of Advanced Study in Algeria in 1980 and a Doctorate degree specializing in Bayram al-Tunisi from the University of Algeria.

Throughout his career, Al-Jaberi held various educational and cultural positions, including teacher in Al-Wardiya District in Tunis, high school teacher in Monastir, director of literature in the Ministry of Culture, and director of the Tunisian Cultural Center in Tripoli. In 1979, he became a member of the Arab League Educational, Cultural and Scientific Organization (ALECSO), where he supervised the cultural department until his retirement in 2000. After retirement, he was appointed head of an encyclopedic project about notable Arab and Muslim figures and scholars, with nineteen volumes published during his oversight.
achievements: Authored multiple novels including 'Laylatul Sanawatil ʿAshr' which was chosen among the 100 best Arabic novels
Supervised cultural department at ALECSO for over 20 years
Led encyclopedic project on notable Arab and Muslim figures with 19 published volumes
Wrote extensively on Maghreb literature and culture

figures.name: Jaber Al Jalahma
figures.title: Tribal Chief
figures.roleDescription: Chief of the Al Jalahma family who was killed by Al Khalifa forces
figures.significance: He was the chief of the Al Jalahma family who was killed during conflicts over revenue distribution in Zubarah.
figures.biography:
Jaber Al Jalahma was the chief of the Al Jalahma family during the 18th century when they were part of the Bani Utbah tribal confederation in Zubarah. The Al Jalahma family, allied under the Bani Utbah tribe, had joined Al Khalifa in Zubarah after migrating from Kuwait, leaving Al Sabah as the sole proprietors of Kuwait.

However, disagreements arose between the Al Khalifa and Al Jalahma over the distribution of revenues from their activities in Zubarah. This led Al Jalahma to move to Ar Ru'ays, just east of Zubarah. The conflict escalated when Al Khalifa attacked them there and killed their chief Jaber Al Jalahma, marking a significant moment in the internal struggles within the Bani Utbah confederation.

figures.name: Rahmah bin Jabir Al Jalahma
figures.title: Leader and Naval Commander
figures.roleDescription: Leader of Al Jalahma clan based in Khor Hassan who opposed Al Khalifa through piracy and military alliances
figures.significance: He was a prominent opponent of Al Khalifa rule, conducting piracy against their ships and supporting their enemies until his death in a dramatic battle in 1826.
figures.biography:
Rahmah bin Jabir Al Jalahma was the leader of the Al Jalahma clan based in Khor Hassan during the late 18th and early 19th centuries. He held a deep grudge against the Al Khalifa family, likely stemming from earlier conflicts within the Bani Utbah confederation, including the killing of his predecessor Jaber Al Jalahma by Al Khalifa forces.

Rahmah bin Jabir actively opposed Al Khalifa rule through maritime warfare and political alliances. He pirated their ships and consistently supported their enemies in various conflicts. His opposition was significant enough to pose a continuous threat to Al Khalifa authority and trade interests. His resistance came to an end in 1826 when he was killed in what contemporary sources described as a dramatic battle, finally removing a persistent thorn in the side of Al Khalifa rule.
achievements: Led sustained maritime resistance against Al Khalifa rule
Maintained Al Jalahma autonomy in Khor Hassan

figures.name: Lulwa bint Abdulla bin Sulayman Al Jalahma
figures.title: Shaikha
figures.roleDescription: Second wife of Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, ruler of Bahrain
figures.significance: She was the second wife of Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, who ruled Bahrain from 1932 to 1942, representing a marriage alliance between the Al Khalifa and Al Jalahma families.
figures.biography:
Shaikha Lulwa bint Abdulla bin Sulayman Al Jalahma was a member of the prominent Al Jalahma family who became the second wife of Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, who would later become the ruler of Bahrain from 1932 to 1942. Her marriage to Hamad represented a significant political alliance between the Al Khalifa ruling family and the Al Jalahma family, which had historically been both allies and rivals within the broader Bani Utbah tribal confederation.

This marriage alliance was part of the complex web of relationships that characterized Gulf politics in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, where intermarriage between prominent families served to strengthen political bonds and resolve historical grievances. Through her marriage to the future ruler, she became part of the ruling household during a crucial period in Bahrain's history, including the discovery of oil and the modernization of the country's administration.

figures.name: Tathi bint Mishan Al Faisal Al Jarba
figures.title: Wife of King Abdullah
figures.roleDescription: Wife of Saudi King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz
figures.significance: Mother of six children of King Abdullah of Saudi Arabia, including Prince Mishaal and Prince Turki.
figures.biography:
Tathi bint Mishan Al Faisal Al Jarba was one of the wives of King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz Al Saud of Saudi Arabia. She belonged to the prominent Al Jarba family of the Iraqi branch of the Shammar tribe. Her marriage to Abdullah was part of traditional tribal alliance-building between the Saudi royal family and major tribal confederations.

Tathi gave birth to six children with King Abdullah, including notable sons Prince Mishaal, who served as governor of the Makkah Province between 2013 and 2015, and Prince Turki, who was a pilot in the Royal Saudi Air Force and served as governor of the Riyadh Province from 2014 to 2015. She also had a daughter Princess Oraib. Through her children, Tathi's lineage became integrated into the Saudi royal family, strengthening ties between the Al Saud and the Shammar confederation.
achievements: Mother of Saudi princes including governors of major provinces

figures.name: Malka bint Saud bin Zaid Al Jarba Al Choumri
figures.title: Wife of King Abdullah
figures.roleDescription: Wife of Saudi King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz
figures.significance: Another wife of King Abdullah from the Al Jarba family, mother of three royal children including Prince Saad and Prince Sultan.
figures.biography:
Malka bint Saud bin Zaid Al Jarba Al Choumri was another wife of King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz Al Saud from the prominent Al Jarba family of the Shammar tribe. Her marriage to Abdullah represented the continued alliance between the Saudi royal family and the influential Shammar confederation.

Malka had three children with King Abdullah: Prince Saad, Princess Sahab, and Prince Sultan. Her marriage demonstrates the practice of multiple marriages within the same tribal family to strengthen political and tribal alliances. The Al Jarba family's connection to the Saudi royal house through multiple marriages reflects their significant status within the broader tribal hierarchy of the Arabian Peninsula.
achievements: Mother of three Saudi royal children

figures.name: Muslit bin Mutlag Al-Jarba
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Shammar tribal leader killed in Wahhabi conflicts
figures.significance: His death in 1791 triggered a major migration of Shammar clans from Najd to Iraq under Al-Jarba leadership.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Muslit bin Mutlag Al-Jarba was a prominent leader of the Shammar tribe during the late 18th century when the tribe faced increasing pressure from Wahhabi expansion in the Arabian Peninsula. He led the tribe during a period of intense conflict with the emerging Saudi state and their Wahhabi allies.

Muslit's death in 1791 during the Wahhabi wars marked a turning point for the Shammar confederation. His killing triggered a major exodus of Shammar clans from their traditional territories in Najd to safer regions in southern Iraq. Under the leadership of the Al-Jarba family, particularly Mutlag Al-Jarba, large numbers of Shammar migrated northward to escape continued Wahhabi pressure and raids. This migration established the foundation for the significant Shammar presence in Iraq that continues to this day, where they became one of the country's largest tribal confederations.
achievements: Led Shammar resistance against early Wahhabi expansion

figures.name: Mutlag Al-Jarba
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Leader of Shammar migration to Iraq
figures.significance: Led the major Shammar exodus from Arabia to Iraq in the 1790s following Wahhabi conflicts.
figures.biography:
Mutlag Al-Jarba was a crucial figure in Shammar tribal history who led the great migration of the tribe from their ancestral lands in Najd to Iraq in the 1790s. Following the death of Muslit bin Mutlag Al-Jarba in 1791, Mutlag took leadership of the Shammar and guided them to safer territories in southern Iraq to escape the advancing Wahhabi forces of the first Saudi state.

Mutlag successfully established Shammar settlements in southern Iraq and organized the tribe's resistance against continued Saudi raids. However, his leadership was cut short when he was killed in 1795 during the Battle of Alabyadh, some sources suggest, as Saudi forces continued their attacks even after the Shammar had relocated. His death marked the end of the initial phase of Shammar settlement in Iraq, but the foundation he laid enabled the tribe to become one of Iraq's most influential tribal confederations under subsequent Al-Jarba leadership.
achievements: Led the great Shammar migration to Iraq
Established Shammar presence in Mesopotamia

figures.name: Faris bin Mohammad Al-Jarba
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Shammar leader in early 19th century Iraq
figures.significance: Ruled over 12,000 Shammar families in Iraq and successfully defeated the Al-Ubaid tribe in 1805.
figures.biography:
Faris bin Mohammad Al-Jarba emerged as a prominent leader of the Iraqi Shammar in the early 19th century, following in the footsteps of earlier Al-Jarba chiefs who had led the tribe's migration from Arabia. He took leadership after Faris Al-Jarba and commanded significant influence over the large Shammar population that had settled in the Mesopotamian region.

In 1805, Faris achieved a decisive military victory by defeating the Al-Ubaid tribe, which significantly strengthened Shammar control over their territories in the Jazirah plain between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers. By 1836, he ruled over approximately 12,000 Shammar families, making him one of the most powerful tribal leaders in Iraq. His leadership helped consolidate Shammar power in their new homeland and established them as a dominant force in Iraqi tribal politics.
achievements: Defeated Al-Ubaid tribe in 1805
Ruled over 12,000 Shammar families

figures.name: Ahmad Jarba
figures.title: Political Opposition Leader
figures.roleDescription: Syrian National Council member and founder of Syria's Tomorrow Movement
figures.significance: Founded Syria's Tomorrow Movement in 2016 and served as a key Syrian opposition leader during the civil war.
figures.biography:
Ahmad Jarba is a prominent Syrian opposition political leader and member of the Syrian National Council who founded the Syria's Tomorrow Movement in March 2016 in Cairo. The party was backed by Egypt and the United Arab Emirates and cooperates with the National Coalition for Syrian Revolutionary and Opposition Forces, although it maintains independence from the coalition. Jarba also works with the Syrian Democratic Council of the Autonomous Administration of North and East Syria.

The Syria's Tomorrow Movement under Jarba's leadership aims to decentralize Syria and implement pluralist democracy while opposing any partition of the state. The party considers all ethnic groups, including Alawites, to be essential components of the Syrian people and calls for greater inclusion in the opposition. In September 2016, Jarba met with Kurdish leaders to form agreements for governing northeastern Syria, and his movement signed cooperation agreements with various Syrian opposition groups including the National Coordination Committee for Democratic Change.
achievements: Founded Syria's Tomorrow Movement
Syrian National Council member
Negotiated Syrian opposition agreements

figures.biography:
Wasmiyah bint Abdul Rahman Al Mu'ammar was a member of the prominent Al Mu'ammar merchant family. She married Prince Bandar bin Abdulaziz, a son of Saudi Arabia's founder King Abdulaziz. Their eldest son, Prince Faisal, was born in 1945 and went on to have a distinguished career in Saudi government service.

As the mother of Prince Faisal bin Bandar, she was connected to the highest levels of Saudi governance through her son's roles as deputy governor and later governor of Asir Province, governor of Al-Qassim Province for over two decades, and eventually governor of Riyadh Province from 2015.

figures.name: Uthman ibn Mu'ammar
figures.title: Emir of Uyayna
figures.roleDescription: 18th century ruler of Uyayna who supported Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab's reforms
figures.significance: Emir of Uyayna who implemented the religious reforms of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, including the destruction of venerated tombs, before being forced to expel the reformer in 1744.
figures.biography:
Uthman ibn Mu'ammar was the emir (ruler) of Uyayna in central Arabia during the 18th century, when the town had become the leading settlement of the Nejd region. When the Islamic reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab returned to Uyayna in 1740 and began preaching against what he considered religious innovations, Ibn Mu'ammar was convinced to implement these ideas.

Under Ibn Mu'ammar's authority, the tomb of Zayd ibn al-Khattab, brother of the second Muslim Caliph Omar, was leveled as part of the Wahhabi campaign against the veneration of Muslim saints. However, in 1744, the Banu Khalid tribe, who held considerable influence over Uyayna from their base in eastern Arabia, compelled Ibn Mu'ammar to expel Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab from the town. The reformer subsequently found refuge with the Al Saud clan in nearby Dir'iyyah, leading to the eventual Saudi conquest of the region.
achievements: Implemented Wahhabi religious reforms in Uyayna
Ordered the destruction of venerated tombs

figures.biography: Sheikh Theyab bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan is the brother of Crown Prince Khaled bin Mohamed Al Nahyan and son of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. He serves as chairman of the Abu Dhabi Crown Prince's Court and is a member of the Executive Council. He also holds the position of chairman of the Early Childhood Authority, Abu Dhabi Transport Company, and Etihad Rail, demonstrating his role in key infrastructure and development projects in Abu Dhabi. He is married to Sheikha Latifa bint Hamdan Al Nahyan and they have three children together: Fatima, Salama, and Zayed.

figures.biography: Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan is a member of the ruling Al Nahyan family and the wife of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. Her father, Sheikh Hamdan bin Mohammed Al Nahyan, was a former UAE deputy prime minister. She married Mohamed bin Zayed in 1980 and they have nine children together – four sons and five daughters – as well as two adopted daughters. She is known for her philanthropic activities and serves as founder and president of the Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan Foundation based in Abu Dhabi. She also chairs the Abu Dhabi Art Host Committee and holds senior positions including chair of the General Women's Union, president of the Supreme Council for Motherhood and Childhood, and supreme chair of the Family Development Foundation. She is a significant art collector, owning works by Pablo Picasso, Rudolf Ernst, and Damien Hirst.
achievements: Founder of Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan Foundation

figures.biography: Sheikh Hamdan bin Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan was born in 1988 and is one of the sons of President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan and Salama bint Hamdan Al Nahyan. He married Sheikha Fakhra bint Khalifa Al Nahyan in 2021, connecting him to another branch of the Al Nahyan family. They have two sons together: Mohammed (born 15 August 2022) and Khalifa (born 28 February 2024). As a member of the next generation of the Al Nahyan family, he represents the future leadership of the emirate.

He was mentioned as having competed for the position of Crown Prince before it was ultimately given to Khaled bin Mohamed by his father. Instead, Tahnoun was appointed to oversee a sovereign wealth fund worth $1.5 trillion, demonstrating his continued importance in the UAE's financial and economic affairs. He has been involved in various diplomatic and intelligence activities.

figures.name: Ali bin Majid bin Ali Hassan Al Majid Al-Naimi
figures.nameAr: علي ماجد علي حسن الماجد النعيمي
figures.title: Bahraini Lawyer and Politician
figures.roleDescription: Member of Bahrain's Council of Representatives
figures.significance: Became the first person to win election as a deputy in the House of Representatives while his father held a ministerial portfolio.
figures.biography:
Ali bin Majid bin Ali Hassan Al Majid Al-Naimi is a Bahraini lawyer and politician who was sworn into the Council of Representatives on December 12, 2018, representing the Seventh District of the Southern Governorate. He earned a master's degree from the Applied Science University in 2012 and followed it up with a doctorate from the Institute of Arab Research and Studies (IARS), a division of the Arab League's Arab League Educational, Cultural and Scientific Organization. His dissertation was a comparative study of the state's responsibility for public servants' mistakes.

In the 2018 Bahraini general election, Al-Naimi ran to represent the Seventh District in the Southern Governorate in the Council of Representatives. He received 1,956 votes for 28.07% in the first round on November 24, necessitating a runoff on December 1, in which he defeated his opponent, Ahmed Al-Tamimi, with 3,267 votes for 52.81%. His election was historically significant as he became the first person to win election as a deputy in the House of Representatives while his father, Majid bin Ali Al-Naimi, held a ministerial portfolio as Minister of Education.

His career has been marked by controversy, including a 2016 report from the Financial and Administrative Control Department showing his absence for seven unaccounted months from work at the National Institute for Human Rights during which he earned BD17,542 in salaries. During the 2018 Bahrain forged degree scandal, his doctorate's provenance from the IARS came under scrutiny, as the Ministry of Education had issued a statement in 2010 stating that degrees from the institute would not be recognized.
achievements: First person to win election as deputy while father held ministerial position

Majid bin Ali Al-Naimi is a Bahraini academic who has served as Minister of Education since 2002. He is the father of Ali bin Majid Al-Naimi, who made history in 2018 by becoming the first person to win election as a deputy in Bahrain's House of Representatives while his father held a ministerial portfolio.

During his long tenure as Minister of Education, Al-Naimi has overseen significant developments in Bahrain's educational sector. His ministry was involved in the 2018 controversy regarding degrees from the Institute of Arab Research and Studies (IARS), where the ministry had issued a statement in 2010 directing applicants to avoid registering at the institute due to significant reservations about its accreditation.

He holds multiple important positions within Ajman and nationally. He serves as Chairman of Ajman Municipality and Planning, overseeing urban development and infrastructure projects in the emirate. Additionally, he is the Chief of Ajman Club and heads R Holding company. He is also the owner of City University College of Ajman, contributing to the emirate's educational sector. At the national level, he serves as President of the UAE Football Association (UAEFA), playing a significant role in the development of football across the United Arab Emirates.

figures.biography: Sheikha Fatima bint Rashid Al Nuaimi was the sister of the current ruler of Ajman, Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III. She died on 14 December 2014. Her significance extends beyond Ajman as she was the mother of Sheikh Hamad Al Sharqi, who is the ruler of Fujairah, another emirate in the UAE. This family connection represents important inter-emirate relationships within the UAE's ruling families, as her son rules one emirate while her brother rules another. Her only son's position as ruler of Fujairah demonstrates the interconnected nature of the UAE's royal families.

figures.biography: Rashid Abdullah Al Nuaimi is a member of the ruling family of Ajman and served as the former foreign minister of the United Arab Emirates. He holds a bachelor's degree in petroleum engineering, which he received from the University of Cairo in 1967. He started his career at the department of oil and industrial affairs in the Emirate of Abu Dhabi before joining the Emirati ministry of foreign affairs, where he worked in various capacities until 1975. In 1975, he was named as the director of political affairs department at the foreign ministry, and in 1976, he became undersecretary for foreign affairs. He served as the minister of state for foreign affairs from 1977 to 1990, when Hamdan bin Zayed succeeded him. In recognition of his diplomatic service, he received the Order of the Two Niles from Sudan in 2018 and the Grand Cordon of the Order of the Rising Sun from Japan in 2024.

figures.biography:
Sheikha Lubna bint Khalid bin Sultan Al Qasimi was born on 4 February 1962 and is an Emirati politician and member of the ruling family of Sharjah, niece to Sheikh Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi. She graduated from California State University, Chico with a bachelor's degree in Computer Science and has an Executive MBA from the American University of Sharjah.

After returning to the UAE in 1981, she worked as a programmer for software company Datamation, then became Dubai branch manager for the General Information Authority responsible for automating the federal government. She later worked for seven years in the Dubai Ports Authority before entering ministerial roles. She served as Minister of State for Tolerance, Minister of State for International Cooperation, and Minister of Economic and Planning of the UAE, making history as the first woman to hold a ministerial post in the UAE.

Since 2014, she has served as President of Zayed University. She has received numerous honors including being named the 36th most powerful woman in the world by Forbes and receiving an honorary Dame Commander of The Most Excellent Order of the British Empire in 2013.

Sheikh Sultan bin Ahmed Al Qasimi is a member of the ruling family of the Emirate of Sharjah and currently serves as Deputy Ruler of Sharjah. He holds a Bachelor of Science degree in Business Administration from Arkansas State University and earned a Master of Science in Computer Information Systems from University of Detroit Mercy.

In December 2021, he was appointed President of the University of Sharjah by the ruler Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi. He serves as Deputy Chairman of The Executive Council, chairman of the Sharjah National Oil Corporation, chairman of Sharjah Media Council, and chairman for Sharjah Petroleum Council. In the private sector, he has chaired Basma Group since 2005, Tilal Properties LLC since 2014, and Arada since 2017, among other leadership positions.

He founded the online news website Sharjah 24 in 2015 and launched OMNES Media in 2017. He is married to his cousin Sheikha Bodour bint Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi, daughter of the ruler of Sharjah. He has received several awards including The Most Outstanding Media Personality Award from the League of Arab States in 2016.

figures.name: Khalid bin Mohammed Al Qasimi
figures.title: Sheikh, 9th Ruler of Sharjah
figures.roleDescription: Ruler of Sharjah and founding father of the UAE
figures.significance: He was a founding father of the United Arab Emirates and the 9th ruler of Sharjah until his assassination in 1972.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Khalid bin Mohammed Al Qasimi was born in Sharjah in 1931 and served as the 9th ruler of the Emirate of Sharjah from 1965 until his murder in 1972. He became ruler following the exile of his Arab nationalist cousin Sheikh Saqr bin Sultan Al Qasimi, who was removed with unanimous consent of the ruling family under British pressure. His status as ruler was confirmed by William Luce, the British Political Resident of the Persian Gulf, on 25 June 1965.

As a quiet and unassuming man, Khalid established Sharjah's first formal police force and played a key role in the negotiations that led to the foundation of the United Arab Emirates on 2 December 1971, making him a founding father of the nation. He was responsible for the demolition of Sharjah Fort in an attempt to erase the memory of his predecessor Saqr, though his brother Sultan convinced him to stop the demolition partway through.

On 24 January 1972, Saqr returned from exile in Egypt with mercenaries and seized power in a coup attempt, invading the Ruler's palace at approximately 2:30 PM. Despite being besieged by the Union Defence Force and Saqr's eventual surrender to UAE Minister of Defence Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, Sheikh Khalid was killed in the action on 25 January 1972. He was succeeded by his brother Sheikh Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi.
achievements: Founding father of the United Arab Emirates
Established Sharjah's first formal police force

figures.name: Khalid bin Saqr Al Qasimi
figures.title: Sheikh, Former Crown Prince of Ras Al Khaimah
figures.roleDescription: Former Crown Prince and Deputy Ruler of Ras Al Khaimah
figures.significance: He served as Crown Prince of Ras Al Khaimah for 45 years before being removed in 2003 due to political differences.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Khalid bin Saqr Al Qasimi was born in 1940 and is the eldest son of Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi, who ruled Ras Al Khaimah from 1948 until 2010. He was named Crown Prince and Deputy Ruler of Ras Al Khaimah in 1958 at age 18 and served in this capacity for 45 years until his removal in June 2003. He completed his elementary schooling in Ras Al Khaimah, secondary education in Cairo, and higher education at Loughborough University in England.

As Crown Prince, he oversaw day-to-day government operations and served as principal contact with foreign governments, leading diplomatic missions to the United States during the Nixon, Reagan and Clinton Administrations. In 1999, President Clinton personally received him in the White House. He served as Chairman of the Board of several major institutions including the National Bank of Ras Al Khaimah, RAK National Oil Company, and RAK Cement Company, promoting economic development including airport and port expansion.

After 37 years in office, he was removed as Crown Prince on June 14, 2003, by his father and replaced by his younger half-brother Sheikh Saud bin Saqr al Qasimi. His removal caused street protests with thousands of demonstrators, requiring Abu Dhabi to send armored vehicles to restore order. Media reports attributed his removal to his opposition to the Iraq War and his vocal hostility to US intervention, which conflicted with UAE federal policy of strengthening cooperation with the US.
achievements: Served as Crown Prince for 45 years (1958-2003)
Led high-level diplomatic missions to the United States
Received by President Clinton in the White House (1999)
Promoted economic development including expansion of International Airport and Saqr Port

figures.name: Saadi Al Munla
figures.title: Former Prime Minister of Lebanon
figures.roleDescription: Lebanese politician and former Prime Minister
figures.significance: Served as Prime Minister of Lebanon in 1946 and held various ministerial positions.
figures.biography:
Saadi Al Munla was born on November 4, 1890, in Tripoli, Lebanon, into a Sunni family. He received a law degree and worked as a lawyer by profession. He was a protégé of Rashid Karami and became a member of the Independence Party headed by Abdul Hamid Karami. He then became a member of the Lebanese parliament.

In 1945, he was appointed minister to the cabinet led by Prime Minister Sami Solh. Munla and two other cabinet members who were also members of the Independence Party, Ahmed Asad and Jamil Talhouk, resigned from office in May 1946. On May 22, 1946, Munla was appointed Prime Minister under President Bishara Khoury, replacing Sami Solh in the post. During his premiership, Munla also assumed the position of economy minister.

Munla remained in office until December 14, 1946, when Riad Solh succeeded him as prime minister. He died in December 1975, having served his country in various political capacities throughout his career.
achievements: Served as Prime Minister of Lebanon (1946)
Served as Minister of Economy
Member of Lebanese Parliament
Member of the Independence Party

figures.title: Mother of Rashidi Emir
figures.roleDescription: Mother of Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid
figures.significance: Mother of the tenth Emir of Jabal Shammar and previously married to Muhammad bin Abdullah Al Rashid.
figures.biography:
Moudi bint Sabhan Al Sabhan was a prominent member of the Al Sabhan family who became connected to the Rashidi dynasty through marriage. She was previously married to Muhammad bin Abdullah Al Rashid before marrying Abdulaziz bin Mutaib Al Rashid, the reigning Rashidi emir. Through her marriage to Abdulaziz, she became the mother of Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid, who would later become the tenth Emir of Jabal Shammar between 1908 and 1920.

Her son Saud was born around 1898, and when his father was killed in the Battle of Rawdat Muhanna in 1906, the Al Sabhan family played a crucial role in protecting and supporting the young prince. Her family's influence in Rashidi politics became evident when her brother Hamoud bin Sabhan Al Sabhan took Saud to safety in Medina and later helped install him as emir in 1908.
achievements: Mother of Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid
Connected Al Sabhan family to Rashidi dynasty

figures.name: Saud bin Saleh Al Sabhan
figures.title: Political conspirator and later minister
figures.roleDescription: Conspirator in assassination of regent and later minister to Emir Saud
figures.significance: Participated in the assassination of regent Zamil bin Salem and later became minister under Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz.
figures.biography:
Saud bin Saleh Al Sabhan was a member of the influential Al Sabhan family who played a significant role in the later period of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar. In 1914, he conspired with his cousin Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid to assassinate the regent Zamil bin Salem Al Sabhan. This assassination was motivated by disagreements over foreign policy, particularly Zamil's preference for maintaining relations with Ibn Saud, which conflicted with the emir's pro-Ottoman stance.

After the successful conspiracy and Zamil's death, Saud Al Sabhan became the minister to Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid. In this position, he served the young emir during the final years of the Rashidi state, as it faced increasing pressure from Ibn Saud's expanding Saudi kingdom. His role as minister made him one of the key Al Sabhan family members in the administration of the emirate during its declining years.
achievements: Participated in political conspiracy that changed emirate leadership
Served as minister to the last effective Rashidi emir

figures.name: Norah bint Hammud Al Sabhan
figures.title: Queen consort of Saudi Arabia
figures.roleDescription: Widow of Rashidi emir who became wife of King Abdulaziz Al Saud
figures.significance: Former widow of Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid who became the eighth wife of King Abdulaziz Al Saud.
figures.biography:
Norah bint Hammud Al Sabhan was originally married to Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid, the tenth and last effective emir of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar. After her husband was assassinated by his cousin Abdullah bin Talal in 1920, she became one of his widows during the final collapse of the Rashidi state.

Following the Saudi conquest of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar in 1921, Norah remarried into the victorious Saudi royal family, becoming the eighth wife of King Abdulaziz Al Saud (Ibn Saud). This marriage represented the integration of prominent Rashidi and Al Sabhan family members into the new Saudi state structure, and was likely part of Ibn Saud's policy of consolidating his rule by incorporating former rival elites through marriage alliances.
achievements: Became queen consort to the founder of modern Saudi Arabia
Represented Al Sabhan family integration into Saudi state

figures.name: Zamil bin Sabhan
figures.title: Early supporter of Rashidi dynasty
figures.roleDescription: Close friend and supporter of the founding Rashidi princes
figures.significance: Early ally who supported the Rashidi brothers in their rise to power in Ha'il.
figures.biography:
Zamil bin Sabhan was an early and influential supporter of the Rashidi dynasty during its founding period in the 1830s. He was described as a cousin and close friend of the founding Rashidi princes Abdullah bin Ali Al Rashid and his brother Prince Ubayd Al Rashid, who led a revolt against the ruler of Ha'il, Muhammad bin Ali of the Jaafar al-Shammari lineage.

Zamil provided crucial support to both brothers during their successful takeover of Ha'il and the establishment of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar in 1836. His backing was particularly important when Abdullah demanded from his brother an ahd (covenant) according to which succession to the office of emir would remain in Abdullah's line. This early support established the Al Sabhan family as key allies and eventually regents of the Rashidi state.
achievements: Supported the founding of the Rashidi dynasty
Helped establish the succession covenant

figures.name: Ibrahim bin Salem Al Sabhan
figures.title: Temporary Emir of Ha'il
figures.roleDescription: Appointed by Ibn Saud as temporary ruler after Saudi conquest
figures.significance: First post-conquest ruler of Ha'il appointed by Ibn Saud to ensure loyalty from local tribes.
figures.biography:
Ibrahim bin Salem Al Sabhan was appointed as the temporary emir of Ha'il by King Abdulaziz Al Saud (Ibn Saud) following the Saudi conquest of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar in 1921. After Ibn Saud gave orders to destroy the historic Barzan Palace and ordered the leaders of both the Rashidi dynasty and Al Sabhan dynasty to relocate from Ha'il to Riyadh, he strategically appointed Ibrahim from the Al Sabhan family to govern the city.

This appointment was a calculated political move by Ibn Saud to ensure the loyalty of the Ha'il people and the Shammar tribe by placing a respected local figure from an established family in charge. Ibrahim's selection represented Ibn Saud's policy of incorporating former regional elites into his administration to maintain stability and legitimacy in newly conquered territories. His role as temporary emir helped facilitate the transition from Rashidi to Saudi rule in the northern Arabian region.
achievements: First Saudi-appointed ruler of Ha'il
Facilitated transition from Rashidi to Saudi rule

As a member of the ruling family of Fujairah, she represents the younger generation of Emirati royalty who have received international education while maintaining their cultural heritage and family traditions.

Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born in 1987 as the second son of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah. He holds a PhD in economics from London, reflecting the family's emphasis on advanced education and expertise.

He currently serves as the head of the media department of Fujairah, playing an important role in the emirate's communications and public relations efforts. His academic background in economics and his media responsibilities position him as a key figure in Fujairah's modern development and international outreach.

She currently heads various charities in Fujairah, demonstrating the royal family's commitment to social responsibility and community welfare. Her charitable work contributes to the social development of the emirate and reflects the traditional values of Arabian leadership in caring for the community.

She has pursued a career as a fashion designer, representing the creative and entrepreneurial spirit of the younger generation of Emirati royalty.

Her work in fashion design showcases the modern aspirations of the Al Sharqi family while maintaining their cultural identity. She represents the diversification of interests and careers among contemporary Emirati royal family members.

figures.biography:
Sheikh Maktoum bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born in 1991 as the youngest son of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah. He is a graduate of the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst, the prestigious British military academy that has trained many members of royal families and military leaders worldwide.

His military education at Sandhurst reflects the family's tradition of service and leadership, as well as their strong ties with British institutions. As the youngest male member of the immediate ruling family, he represents the continuation of the Al Sharqi dynasty's commitment to professional excellence and international education.

Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born on December 29, 2009, as the eldest son of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi and Sheikha Latifa bint Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum. He is the grandson of the current ruler of Fujairah and represents the future generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty.

As the eldest son of the Crown Prince, he is positioned in the line of succession for the emirate. His lineage connects him to both the Al Sharqi family of Fujairah and the Al Maktoum family of Dubai, strengthening inter-emirate family ties within the UAE.

As a member of the younger generation of the Al Sharqi family, she represents the continuation of the dynasty and the strong family ties between the ruling families of Fujairah and Dubai through her mother's lineage.

As a member of the fourth generation of the modern Al Sharqi dynasty, she represents the future of the ruling family and maintains the strong inter-emirate connections through her maternal lineage to the Al Maktoum family of Dubai.

His name honors the family tradition, being named after his uncle Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi. As a member of the youngest generation of the Al Sharqi family, he represents the continuation of the dynasty into the future.

figures.significance: Current Deputy Emir of Qatar and unofficial heir presumptive, appointed by his half-brother Emir Tamim in 2014.
figures.biography:
Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani was born on 9 February 1988 in Doha, Qatar, as the son of former Emir Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani and his third wife Sheikha Noora bint Khalid Al Thani. He graduated with a Bachelor of Science in Foreign Service from Georgetown University in 2010, demonstrating the family's commitment to international education and diplomacy.

From 20 December 2011 to 10 November 2014, Abdullah served as chief of the Amiri Diwan of the State of Qatar, gaining experience in government administration. On 11 November 2014, his half-brother Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani appointed him to the post of Deputy Emir, unofficially making him heir to the throne of Qatar, though it is expected that Emir Tamim will eventually appoint one of his own sons as crown prince.

In his current roles, Abdullah serves as Chairman of the Board of Trustees of QatarEnergy since 4 November 2018, Chairman of Board of Regents of Qatar University, and Vice-chairman of the Supreme Council for Economic Affairs and Investment. He married Sheikha Al Maha bint Muhammad Al Attiyah at Al Wajbah Palace on 22 March 2012.

figures.significance: Current consort of the Emir of Qatar and prominent member of the royal family, representing Qatar in official state functions.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Jawaher bint Hamad bin Suhaim Al Thani was born in Doha in 1984 to Sheikh Hamad bin Suhaim Al Thani, a government minister and member of the Qatari royal family. She is a granddaughter of former Emir Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani, making her a member of the royal family by birth. Her paternal grandfather, Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad Al Thani, served as Qatar's foreign minister.

She married her first cousin, Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani, on 8 January 2005 at Al Wajbah Palace, before he became Emir. They have four children together: Sheikha Al Mayassa (born 2006), Sheikh Hamad (born 2008), Sheikha Aisha (born 2010), and Sheikh Jassim (born 2012). When her husband succeeded as Emir in 2013, she became his consort.

As consort, Sheikha Jawaher has accompanied her husband on official state visits, including to Spain (2022), the Netherlands (2024), and attended major international events such as Queen Elizabeth II's funeral (2022) and King Charles III's coronation (2023). She serves as royal patron for Qatar University's female graduates and received a Master of Arts degree from Qatar University in 2021. She was awarded the Spanish Order of Isabella the Catholic in 2022.

figures.biography: Abd Allah ibn Muhammad Abd al-Wahhab was the son of the founder Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and became leader of the Saudi religious establishment following his father's death in 1791. He endorsed further Al Saud expansion and wrote a number of tracts against Shia belief, continuing his father's religious mission. His leadership came during a critical period when the Saudi state was expanding but also facing growing Ottoman opposition. The Ottoman forces from Egypt eventually destroyed the Al Saud state in 1818, which was also a disaster for the Al ash-Sheikh family, though Abd Allah had died by this time.
