figures.name: Abd Allah ibn Abd al-Latif Al ash-Sheikh
figures.nameAr: عبد الله بن عبد اللطيف آل الشيخ
figures.title: Head of Saudi Religious Establishment
figures.roleDescription: Religious leader during the early years of Abdul Aziz Al Saud's rule
figures.significance: Led the religious establishment during the transition period and strengthened ties with the returning Al Saud through marriage alliance.
figures.biography: Abd Allah ibn Abd al-Latif Al ash-Sheikh was a descendant of Ibn Abd al-Wahhab who led the ulema during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. When the Al Saud were defeated by the Al Rashid in 1891 and went into exile, he decided to side with the Al-Rashid and moved to Ha'il, indicating that preservation of the Wahhabi cause took precedence over the family compact with the Al Saud. However, when Abdul Aziz Al Saud returned from exile in 1902 and re-established the Saudi state around Riyadh, Abd Allah changed sides again and re-joined the Al Saud. Abdul Aziz accepted this change of heart and cemented the relationship with the Al ash-Sheikh through matrimonial links, including marrying Abd Allah's daughter. He was the father of Tarfa bint Abdullah, who became one of Abdul Aziz's wives and mother of King Faisal.
achievements: Led religious establishment during Al Saud restoration
Strengthened Al ash-Sheikh alliance through marriage ties
Father of Tarfa bint Abdullah, mother of King Faisal

figures.name: Abdullah ibn Muhammad Al ash-Sheikh
figures.nameAr: عبد الله بن محمد آل الشيخ
figures.title: Chairman of Majlis ash-Shura
figures.roleDescription: Minister of Justice (1992-2009) and Chairman of Consultative Assembly since 2009
figures.significance: Served as Saudi Minister of Justice for 17 years and implemented major judicial reforms including the creation of specialized courts.
figures.biography: Abdullah ibn Muhammad Al ash-Sheikh was born in Diriyah in 1948 and is a member of the noted Al ash-Sheikh family of religious scholars. He was educated by his father, Muhammad ibn Ibrahim Al ash-Sheikh, the former Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia. He graduated with a bachelor's degree in Sharia from the Shariah College in Riyadh in 1975, then studied at Al-Azhar University in Cairo before returning to earn his PhD in Fiqh from Imam Mohammed bin Saudi University in 1987. After obtaining his doctorate, he became a lecturer and professor until his appointment as Minister of Justice in November 1992, replacing Mohammed bin Jubair. During his tenure, he drew up the judiciary law of 2007 on the King's instructions, creating specialized courts and a Supreme Court. He was replaced as justice minister in February 2009 in a cabinet reshuffle aimed at replacing conservative incumbents with more progressive candidates, and was appointed Chairman of the Majlis ash-Shura the same year.
achievements: Saudi Minister of Justice (1992-2009)
Created judiciary law of 2007 establishing specialized courts
Chairman of Majlis ash-Shura since 2009

figures.name: Tarfa bint Abdullah Al Sheikh
figures.nameAr: طرفة بنت عبد الله آل الشيخ
figures.title: Queen Consort
figures.roleDescription: Wife of King Abdulaziz and mother of King Faisal
figures.significance: Mother of King Faisal of Saudi Arabia and daughter of prominent religious leader Abd Allah ibn Abd al-Latif.
figures.biography: Tarfa bint Abdullah Al Sheikh was born in 1884, the daughter of Abdullah bin Abdullatif Al Sheikh, a principal religious teacher and adviser to the Emir of Nejd. She was one of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab's eighth generation direct descendants. Tarfa married Emir Abdulaziz in 1902 immediately after he captured Riyadh, becoming his third wife. This marriage was a strategic move to strengthen the links between the Al Sauds and the Al Sheikhs. Her sisters also married into the Al Saud family - Munira married Abdulaziz's half-brother Muhammad bin Abdul Rahman, and Sara married his full-brother Saad bin Abdul Rahman. Tarfa and Abdulaziz's first child, Noura, was born in 1904, followed by their son Faisal in April 1906. Tragically, Tarfa died in October 1906 when Faisal was just six months old. Her son Faisal would later become King of Saudi Arabia in 1964.
achievements: Mother of King Faisal of Saudi Arabia
Strengthened Al Saud-Al ash-Sheikh alliance through marriage
Eighth generation descendant of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab

figures.name: Ahmad al-Muhajir
figures.title: Founder of Ba 'Alawi lineage
figures.roleDescription: Ancestor who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut
figures.significance: Foundational ancestor of the Ba 'Alawi sada who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut in 931 (318H).
figures.biography:
Ahmad al-Muhajir is the foundational ancestor of the Ba 'Alawi sada, who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut in 931 (318H). He was a descendant of Muhammad through Husayn ibn Ali. When Ahmad al-Muhajir arrived in Hadhramaut, he maintained close family ties in Basra, where many of his relatives resided. His son Muhammad remained in Basra to oversee the family's property, together with his other sons, Ali and Hussein.

Al-Muhajir's children and grandchildren invested in Hadhramaut for many years derived from their holdings in Iraq. They continued to maintain contact with their ancestral homeland and cousins for a few generations, receiving news and accounts that helped preserve their shared heritage and historical memory. The Ba 'Alawi trace their lineage back to him, and classical scholars of Islam such as Ibn Hajar al-Haytami, Yusuf al-Nabhani and Murtada al-Zabidi have validated the genealogy of the Ba Alawi Sada.
achievements: Founded the Ba 'Alawi lineage in Hadhramaut
Established family presence outside Arabia

figures.name: Alawi bin 'Ubaydillah
figures.title: First descendant named 'Alawi
figures.roleDescription: First of al-Muhajir's descendants to be named 'Alawi
figures.significance: The first of al-Muhajir's descendants to be named 'Alawi, from whom the Ba 'Alawi take their name.
figures.biography:
Alawi bin 'Ubaydillah was the first of Ahmad al-Muhajir's descendants to be named 'Alawi, and from him the Ba 'Alawi family takes its name. He was the grandson of Ahmad al-Muhajir and the first Sayyid to be born in Hadhramaut. He was the only one of Imam al-Muhajir's descendants to produce a continued line in Hadhramaut, as the lines of his other grandsons in Hadhramaut—Basri (originally named Ismāʿīl) and Jadīd—ceased after a few generations.

Accordingly, Imam Al-Muhajir's descendants in Hadhramaut hold the name Bā 'Alawi or Bani 'Alawi ("descendants of Alawi") after him. The origin of the Ba 'Alawi name goes back to this ancestor, and the use of the name Ba 'Alawi came after they were influenced by the Hadhrami way of referring to their fathers, with the meaning of (Ba) among the Hadharem being "children of".
achievements: First descendant to bear the name 'Alawi
Established the continuing Ba 'Alawi line in Hadhramaut

figures.name: Muhammad bin Ali Baalawi
figures.title: Al-Faqih Al-Muqaddam
figures.roleDescription: Founder of the Ba 'Alawi Tariqa
figures.significance: Founded the Ba 'Alawi Tariqa and established the principle of non-violence for Alawi Sayyids.
figures.biography:
Muhammad bin Ali Baalawi, known as "Al-Faqih Al-Muqaddam" (The Foremost Jurist), was the founder of the Ba 'Alawi Tariqa, one of the Sufi orders spread in the Islamic world. During his time, Sayyid families in Hadhramaut were seen as a threat by other tribes, and due to regional instability, it was normal during his study that Muhammad bin Ali put a sword on his lap for protection.

Muhammad grew tired of the tension and bloodshed in the ranks of the believers, thus symbolically broke his sword and announced that his Tariqa and the way of Alawi Sayyids are non-violence and renounced any tariqa that uses violence. This established the peaceful nature of the Ba 'Alawi approach to spreading Islam, which would later influence the dissemination of Islam in Southeast Asia through traders and clerics of Hadhramaut who transited in India since the 15th century.
achievements: Founded the Ba 'Alawi Tariqa
Established the principle of non-violence for the Ba 'Alawi
Created peaceful approach to Islamic teaching

figures.name: Ali bin Alawi
figures.title: Khali' Qasam
figures.roleDescription: Descendant who established Tarim as Ba 'Alawi headquarters
figures.significance: Moved to Tarim in 521 AH and made it the headquarters of the Bani Alawi.
figures.biography:
Ali bin Alawi, known as "Khali' Qasam," was a descendant of Ahmad Al-Muhajir who played a crucial role in establishing the Ba 'Alawi presence in Tarim. In 521 AH, he moved to the city of Tarim and made it his home for himself and his children, following the family's earlier movements through various villages in the valley.

After Ahmad Al-Muhajir's descendants had moved through the valley's villages, settling in the village of Sumal for a time before moving to the village of Bayt Jubair, Ali bin Alawi's decision to settle in Tarim proved to be historically significant. Since his settlement there, Tarim has become the headquarters of the Bani Alawi to this day, serving as their spiritual and administrative center.
achievements: Established Tarim as the Ba 'Alawi headquarters
Consolidated Ba 'Alawi presence in a major city

figures.name: Al-Ḥabīb ʿUmar bin ʿAbd al-Raḥmān al-ʿAṭṭās
figures.nameAr: الحبيب عمر بن عبد الرحمن العطاس
figures.title: Al-Habib
figures.roleDescription: First Ba 'Alawi to be called Habib
figures.significance: The first person from the Bā ʿAlawī clan to be called Habib, establishing this honorific title.
figures.biography:
Al-Ḥabīb ʿUmar bin ʿAbd al-Raḥmān al-ʿAṭṭās (d. 1072 AH / 1661 CE) holds the distinction of being the first person from the Bā ʿAlawī clan to be called Habib. This represented an evolution in the titles used by Ba 'Alawi scholars and religious leaders over time.

Previously, descendants of Imam Ahmad al-Muhajir who became scholars in Islamic studies were called Imam, then Sheikh, but al-ʿAṭṭās was the first to be honored with the title Habib. This title became an important part of Ba 'Alawi tradition and continues to be used for respected religious leaders within the community.
achievements: First Ba 'Alawi to receive the title 'Habib'
Established new honorific tradition

figures.name: Muhammad 'Alawi al-Maliki
figures.title: Traditional Sunni Islamic Scholar
figures.roleDescription: Foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholar from Saudi Arabia
figures.significance: Considered to be the Mujaddid of 20th-21st century and one of the foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholars.
figures.biography:
Muhammad al-Hasan ibn Alawi ibn al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Aziz, also known as Muhammad ibn Alawi al-Maliki, was born in 1944 in Mecca and became one of the foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholars of contemporary times. The Maliki family is one of the most respected families in Mecca and has produced great scholars who taught in the Haram of Mecca for centuries. Five of his ancestors had been the Maliki Imams of the Haram of Mecca.

With his father's instruction, he studied and mastered the various traditional Islamic sciences of Aqidah, Tafsir, Hadith, Seerah, Fiqh, Usul, Mustalah, Nahw, and received full Ijazah from scholars of Mecca and Medina to teach these sciences to others. In the early 1990s, the Government of Saudi Arabia began supporting practitioners of Sufism in the Hijaz region as a way to counter Wahhabism and bolster religious support of the state; al-Maliki became the self-imposed leader of Hijazi Sufism under state sponsorship, with several thousand supporters. He died in 2004 and was buried in Mecca, with Crown Prince 'Abd Allah stating that al-Maliki "was faithful both to his religion and country."
achievements: Mastered traditional Islamic sciences
Became leader of Hijazi Sufism
Authored numerous religious works
Received state recognition in Saudi Arabia

figures.name: Amrus ibn Yusuf al-Muwallad al-Laridi
figures.title: General and Governor
figures.roleDescription: Muwallad general of the Emirate of Córdoba and governor of Zaragoza
figures.significance: Founded the influential Banu Amrus family and controlled strategic territories in the upper Ebro valley for nearly a decade.
figures.biography:
Amrus ibn Yusuf was a Muwallad (probably of Visigothic origin) general who served the Emirate of Córdoba. A native of Huesca, he and his kinsman Shabrit were mawālī servants of Aysun ibn Sulayman al-Arabi. In 791/792, Amrus turned against his master Matruh al-Arabi during a rebellion, attacking and killing him with swords alongside Sarhabil ibn Saltan al-Zawagi. This act of betrayal brought him favor with the Córdoba court, where he was rewarded with the position of wali of Talavera.

In 802, Amrus was sent from Toledo as a general to suppress another Zaragoza rebellion, successfully taking both Zaragoza and Huesca while expelling Bahlul ibn Marzuq. He established the settlement that would become Tudela, installing his son Yusuf ibn Amrus there. When Zaragoza rebelled again under Fortun ibn Musa in December 802, Amrus was appointed governor of Zaragoza in 803/804, also installing his kinsman Sabrit in Huesca. He successfully quelled a rebellion in Tudela in 807 and occupied the county of Sobrarbe after the death of Oriol of Aragon, territory that remained under his control until Aznar Galíndez I regained it in 814. According to chronicler Al-Udri, Amrus held Zaragoza for nearly ten years, dying in either 808/809 or 813/814.
achievements: Established the settlement that became Tudela
Controlled Zaragoza for nearly a decade
Founded the influential Banu Amrus dynasty
Successfully suppressed multiple rebellions in the upper Ebro valley

figures.name: Mohammed bin Khamis
figures.title: Chief of Al Bidda
figures.roleDescription: Tribal chief and successor to Buhur bin Jubrun
figures.significance: He was a prominent member of the Al-Buainain tribe and successor of Buhur bin Jubrun as chief of Al Bidda, though he was embroiled in controversy for murdering a native of Bahrain.
figures.biography:
Mohammed bin Khamis was a prominent member of the Al-Buainain tribe who succeeded Buhur bin Jubrun as chief of Al Bidda in 1828. His leadership was marked by controversy when he murdered a native of Bahrain, which prompted the Al Khalifa sheikh to imprison him. This incident had significant consequences for the Al-Buainain tribe and the region.

In response to Mohammed bin Khamis's imprisonment, the Al-Buainain tribe revolted against the Al Khalifa. This rebellion provoked a harsh response from the Al Khalifa, who destroyed the tribe's fort and evicted them to Fuwayrit and Ar Ru'ays. This incident allowed the Al Khalifa to gain additional jurisdiction over the town of Al Bidda. With essentially no effective ruler after these events, Al Bidda and the nearby settlement of Doha became a sanctuary for pirates and outlaws, marking a turbulent period in the region's history.

figures.name: Buhur bin Jubrun
figures.title: Ruler and Initial Founder of Doha
figures.roleDescription: Chief of the Al-Buainain tribe and founder
figures.significance: He was the ruler and initial founder of Doha, and chief of the Al-Buainain tribe when the British political resident John MacLeod visited Al Bidda in January 1823.
figures.biography:
Buhur bin Jubrun was the ruler and initial founder of Doha, serving as the chief of the Al-Buainain tribe in the early 19th century. In January 1823, the British political resident John MacLeod visited Al Bidda specifically to meet with Buhur bin Jubrun. During this significant diplomatic encounter, MacLeod noted that Al Bidda was the only substantial trading port in the peninsula at that time, highlighting the importance of the settlement under Buhur bin Jubrun's leadership.

As the founder of Doha, Buhur bin Jubrun played a crucial role in establishing what would become Qatar's capital city. His leadership of the Al-Buainain tribe and control over the important trading port of Al Bidda made him a key figure in the region's early commercial and political development. His position as both tribal chief and town ruler represented the traditional Arabian leadership structure of the time, where tribal authority and territorial control were closely interlinked.
achievements: Founded the city of Doha
Established Al Bidda as the peninsula's main trading port
Conducted diplomatic relations with British officials

figures.biography: Fadhil al-Milani was a respected Shia religious scholar who gained prominence in the religious community. He is mentioned in the sources as the father of a daughter who married Moustafa Al-Qazwini, an Iraqi-American Shia imam who founded several Islamic centers in California. The marriage connection suggests al-Milani held a position of respect within the Shia scholarly community, as such matrimonial alliances were typically formed between families of similar religious and social standing. His daughter's marriage to al-Qazwini, who went on to establish important Islamic institutions in the United States, indicates the al-Milani family's continued influence in Islamic scholarly circles.

figures.name: Mohammed Jaham Abdulaziz Al Kuwari
figures.title: Ambassador and Diplomat
figures.roleDescription: Senior Qatari diplomat and former Ambassador to the United States
figures.significance: Prominent Qatari diplomat who served as Ambassador to the United States from 2014-2016 and previously as Ambassador to France for a decade.
figures.biography:
Mohammed Jaham Al Kuwari was born on 20 May 1958, one of 13 children in his family. He received his bachelor's degree in political science from University of Portland in 1980 and a master's degree in international relations from the University of Madrid in 1990. Al Kuwari began his diplomatic career at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in 1981 as a Third Secretary, first posted to Washington DC from 1981 to 1986, then Madrid from 1986 to 1990.

He held various senior positions including Deputy Head of Cabinet (1993-1995), Director of European and American Affairs (1995-1997 and 2001-2002), and head of delegation for Qatar's political and strategic dialogue with France (2001-2002). Prior to his appointment as Ambassador to the United States, Al Kuwari served for a decade as Qatar's Ambassador to France and was accredited to Switzerland and the Holy See. In 2007, he became non-resident Ambassador to Monaco and Portugal.

In December 2013, Al Kuwari was appointed Ambassador of Qatar to Washington, DC, officially presenting his credentials to President Barack Obama on 10 March 2014 and serving until December 2016. Throughout his career, he has been recognized with numerous international honors including multiple decorations from France's Legion of Honour, the Grand Cross of the Order of St. Gregory the Great from Vatican City, and the Grand Cross of the Order of Isabella the Catholic from Spain.
achievements: Served as Qatar's Ambassador to the United States (2014-2016)
Served as Qatar's Ambassador to France for a decade

figures.name: Ghanim Al Kuwari
figures.title: Minister of Information and Culture
figures.roleDescription: Former Qatari government minister
figures.significance: Served as Qatar's minister of information and culture and was involved in a significant political incident in 1985 related to succession disputes within the ruling family.
figures.biography:
Ghanim Al Kuwari served as Qatar's minister of information and culture during the 1980s. He became involved in a major political crisis in 1985 when Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad Al Thani, brother of Emir Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani, died suddenly of a heart attack in August 1985. Suhaim had been plotting a coup against his brother after learning that Khalifa had named his own son as heir, and maintained his own weapons cache and supporters in northern Qatar.

Following Suhaim's sudden death, his sons blamed Ghanim Al Kuwari for not responding promptly to their father's calls for medical attention during his medical emergency. This accusation led to a serious escalation when Suhaim's sons attempted to assassinate Al Kuwari in retaliation. As a result of this assassination attempt, they were subsequently imprisoned. The incident highlights the complex political dynamics and family rivalries within Qatar's ruling circles during this period.

figures.roleDescription: Current Chief of Staff of the Bahrain Defence Force
figures.significance: He serves as the Chief of Staff of the Bahrain Defence Force, holding the rank of Lieutenant General.
figures.biography:
Lieutenant General Theyab bin Saqer Al Noaimi currently serves as the Chief of Staff of the Bahrain Defence Force (BDF). He holds this senior military position in the BDF, which was established in 1968 and consists of the Royal Bahraini Air Force, Royal Bahraini Army, Royal Bahraini Navy and the Royal Guard.

The BDF numbers about 18,000 personnel and is under the direct command and leadership of a commander-in-chief who holds the rank of field marshal. Al Noaimi's role as Chief of Staff makes him one of the most senior military officers in the Bahraini armed forces, working under the overall command structure that includes Crown Prince Salman bin Hamad Al Khalifa as Deputy Supreme Commander and Field Marshal Khalifa bin Ahmed Al Khalifa as Commander-in-Chief.

figures.name: Essa Abdulla Al Basha Al Noaimi

figures.significance: He received Sudan's second-highest honour, the Order of the Two Niles (Class I: Grand Cordon) in 2009.
figures.biography:
Essa Abdulla Al Basha Al Noaimi is a distinguished figure who was honored by Sudan with the Order of the Two Niles in 2009. He received the First Class Grand Cordon of this prestigious order, which is Sudan's second-highest honour after the Order of the Republic.

The Order of the Two Niles was established on 16 November 1961 during Ibrahim Abboud's military government and is given to both Sudanese and foreign nationals, civilians and military personnel, who have provided great services to the state. Al Noaimi's receipt of this high honor in 2009 indicates his significant contributions, though the specific nature of his services is not detailed in the available records.
achievements: Recipient of Sudan's Order of the Two Niles (Grand Cordon, 2009)

figures.name: Rashid Abdullah Al Nuaimi
figures.title: Former Foreign Minister of the United Arab Emirates
figures.roleDescription: Former UAE Foreign Minister and member of Ajman's ruling family
figures.significance: He served as the UAE's foreign minister and is a member of the ruling Al Nuaimi family of Ajman emirate.
figures.biography:
Rashid Abdullah Al Nuaimi is a member of the ruling family of Ajman, Al Nuaimi, and served as the former foreign minister of the United Arab Emirates. He holds a bachelor's degree in petroleum engineering, which he received from the University of Cairo in 1967.

Nuaimi started his career at the department of oil and industrial affairs in the Emirate of Abu Dhabi before joining the Emirati ministry of foreign affairs. Until 1975, he worked in various capacities at the foreign ministry. In 1975, he was named director of political affairs department at the foreign ministry, and in 1976 became undersecretary for foreign affairs. He served as minister of state for foreign affairs from 1977 to 1990, when Hamdan bin Zayed succeeded him in the post. In recognition of his diplomatic service, he received the Order of the Two Niles from Sudan in 2018 and the Grand Cordon of the Order of the Rising Sun from Japan in 2024.
achievements: Former UAE Foreign Minister
Recipient of Sudan's Order of the Two Niles (2018)
Recipient of Japan's Grand Cordon of the Order of the Rising Sun (2024)

figures.name: Ali Rashid Al Noaimi
figures.title: Vice Chancellor of United Arab Emirates University
figures.roleDescription: Former Al Islah member turned UAE University administrator
figures.significance: He is a former member of the banned Islamist group Al Islah who became Vice Chancellor of UAE University.
figures.biography:
Ali Rashid Al Noaimi serves as the Vice Chancellor of United Arab Emirates University and is a former member of Al Islah, the UAE-based Islamist political group affiliated with the Muslim Brotherhood. Al Noaimi's experience with Al Islah provides insight into the organization's operations and ideology.

As a former Al Islah member who later took a senior academic position, Al Noaimi has been critical of the organization's loyalties. He stated that Al Islah members 'get their orders from outside' and 'they are not loyal to their country,' providing testimony about the group's alleged foreign connections and influence. His transition from involvement with the banned political organization to becoming a senior university administrator reflects the complex political and social dynamics within the UAE's educated elite.
achievements: Vice Chancellor of United Arab Emirates University

figures.biography: Sheikh Kamal al-Qassab served as the director of schools for the Haifa Islamic Society in Palestine during the early 1930s. His dismissal from this position in late 1932 caused significant controversy and led to political ramifications within the Palestinian Arab community. According to the Filastin newspaper, his sacking prompted Rashid al-Haj Ibrahim, a prominent Palestinian Arab leader and banker, to resign from his position in the Haifa Islamic Society in protest. The dismissal was reportedly part of broader political maneuvering, as al-Husayni was allegedly attempting to undermine the rising al-Istiqlal (Independence) party by controlling key institutions like the Islamic Society.
achievements: Served as director of schools for Haifa Islamic Society

figures.biography:
Saad Mohammed Al-Rumaihi was a sports journalist working at the Al Raya newspaper in Qatar during the late 1970s. He was one of the founding members of Al-Gharafa Sports Club, established on June 6, 1979, alongside prominent members of the Al Thani royal family including Sheikh Mohammed bin Jassim Al Thani, Sheikh Hamad bin Jassim Al Thani, Sheikh Hamad bin Faisal Al Thani, and Sheikh Ali bin Abdullah Al Thani.

According to a documentary produced by Al Kass sports channel about the history of Al-Gharafa Sports Club, Sheikh Hamad bin Jassim mentioned that the idea to establish the club was initially suggested by Saad Al-Rumaihi during his time as a sports journalist. His suggestion led to the creation of what would become one of Qatar's most successful football clubs, which has won multiple league championships and domestic cups. The club was founded with the intent of creating sports facilities for the youth in the Al Gharrafa region of Al Rayyan municipality.

Hamda bint Hassan Al-Sulaiti is an appointed member of Qatar's Consultative Assembly and currently serves as Deputy Speaker of the council. She holds one of the key leadership positions in Qatar's legislative body, which consists of 45 members who are directly appointed by the Emir.

As Deputy Speaker, she plays an important role in the functioning of Qatar's legislative process, working alongside the Chairman Hassan bin Abdullah Al-Ghanim. Her position demonstrates the Al-Sulaiti family's continued prominence in Qatar's political establishment and their involvement in the country's governance structures.

figures.name: Rashed Albuflasa
figures.title: Family Member
figures.roleDescription: Brother of Mohamed Albuflasa and family spokesperson
figures.significance: Served as family spokesperson during Mohamed Albuflasa's imprisonment and explained the motivation behind his brother's political activism.
figures.biography:
Rashed Albuflasa is the brother of Mohamed Yousif Rashid Albuflasa and served as a family spokesperson during the 2011 Bahraini uprising. When his brother Mohamed was arrested and became the first political prisoner of the uprising, Rashed explained to the media the significance and motivation behind Mohamed's speech at Pearl Roundabout.

According to Rashed, Mohamed's speech was intended to calm the flames of anger caused by efforts to create disagreement between Shiites and Sunnis. He wanted to show there was no difference between them and that both were Bahraini. Rashed clarified that while the government was surprised and regarded the speech as an attack on them, Mohamed was not against the royal family and government, but rather sought to promote national unity and bridge sectarian divides during a time of significant political tension in Bahrain.

figures.name: Hafiz Jamal ud din
figures.title: Religious Leader and Village Founder
figures.roleDescription: Spiritual figure who founded the village of Bani Hafiz
figures.significance: Founded the village of Bani Hafiz after receiving land as a gift from the Raja of Salmia in the late 1700s.
figures.biography: Hafiz Jamal ud din was a religious and spiritual person who lived in the late 1700s. The land of Bani Hafiz was given as a gift to him by the Raja of Salmia during this period. He used to meditate in the jungle in Bani Hafiz and was known for his spiritual practices. Hafiz Jamal had two sons: Hafiz Muhammad Ghous and Hafiz Muhammad Jee. His elder son Hafiz Muhammad Ghous shifted to Ban Khawra, a small village in Lamnian on the other side of the river Jehlum, while Hafiz Muhammad Jee stayed at Bani Hafiz and his family propagated there. Urs (religious festival) of Hafiz Jamal and his sons are carried out around the year in Bani Hafiz, with people from distant areas coming to celebrate these festivities, indicating his lasting spiritual influence in the region.
achievements: Founded the village of Bani Hafiz
Established a spiritual legacy commemorated through annual Urs festivals

figures.name: Hafiz Muhammad Jee
figures.title: Religious Leader
figures.roleDescription: Son of Hafiz Jamal ud din who continued the family's spiritual tradition
figures.significance: Stayed in Bani Hafiz and propagated the family lineage, becoming the patriarch of the Bani Hafiz family.
figures.biography: Hafiz Muhammad Jee was one of the two sons of Hafiz Jamal ud din, the founder of Bani Hafiz village. While his elder brother Hafiz Muhammad Ghous moved to Ban Khawra across the river Jehlum, Hafiz Muhammad Jee chose to remain in Bani Hafiz. The family of Hafiz Muhammad Jee propagated in Bani Hafiz, making him the key patriarch of the family's continuation in the village. He had three sons: Hafiz Abdul Qudus (known as Molvi Bajee), Hafiz Muhammad Yunus, and Hafiz Abdul Latif, all of whom stayed and prospered in Bani Hafiz. His lineage continued the spiritual traditions of the family, with annual Urs festivals being held to commemorate him alongside his father.
achievements: Established the main family lineage in Bani Hafiz
Father of three prominent sons who continued the family tradition

figures.name: Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar
figures.title: Politician and Acting President
figures.roleDescription: 17th Acting President of Azad Kashmir and prominent political leader
figures.significance: Served as Acting President of Azad Kashmir and was a founding member of Pakistan People's Party in the region.
figures.biography: Born on 12 September 1944 in Bani Hafiz, a village of Hattian Bala, Azad Kashmir, Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar belonged to the spiritual family of Bani Hafiz. His father was Hafiz Abdul Latif, making him the great-grandson of Hafiz Jamal ud din. Born an orphan, he was raised by his uncle Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus, a local Sufi saint. He matriculated from Chikar Tehsil and later went to Karachi to study law, earning a master's degree in political science and Urdu from Karachi University. Before joining politics, he worked as a primary school teacher but left to join Zulfiqar Ali Bhutto's Pakistan People's Party in the early 1970s, becoming one of the founding members in Azad Kashmir. Zaffar served in various high positions including Acting President of Azad Kashmir from 20-29 July 1991, Speaker of AJ&K Legislative Assembly from 1990-1991, and Senior Minister from 1996-2001. He was known for his loyalty to the Bhutto family and accompanied Benazir Bhutto to rallies and election campaigns. He died of a heart attack on 2 September 2006 in Rawalpindi.

Founding member of Pakistan People's Party in Azad Kashmir
Speaker of AJ&K Legislative Assembly
Senior Minister for five years
President of PPP Azad Jammu and Kashmir

figures.name: Hafiz Abdul Latif

figures.significance: One of three sons who stayed and prospered in Bani Hafiz, father of prominent politician Ishaq Zaffar.
figures.biography: Hafiz Abdul Latif was one of the three sons of Hafiz Muhammad Jee who stayed and prospered in Bani Hafiz village. He was the father of Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar, who would later become a prominent politician and Acting President of Azad Kashmir. The family belonged to the spiritual lineage of Bani Hafiz, continuing the religious traditions established by his grandfather Hafiz Jamal ud din. His son Ishaq Zaffar was born an orphan, suggesting that Hafiz Abdul Latif died when his son was very young, leading to the child being raised by his uncle Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus.
achievements: Continued the spiritual family tradition in Bani Hafiz
Father of a prominent political leader

figures.name: Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus
figures.title: Sufi Saint
figures.roleDescription: Local Sufi saint and spiritual leader
figures.significance: Local Sufi saint whose tomb became a shrine in Bani Hafiz village.
figures.biography: Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus was one of the three sons of Hafiz Muhammad Jee who stayed and prospered in Bani Hafiz. He became a prominent local Sufi saint whose spiritual influence extended throughout the region. When his nephew Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar was born an orphan, Hafiz Muhammad Yunus raised him, demonstrating his role as a family patriarch and caretaker. His daughter was Anwar Kasur, who became the principal of degree college for girls Hattian Bala, and the institute was later named after her. His tomb became a shrine in the village, indicating his lasting spiritual significance. His son was Sahibzada Irfan Danish, who served as a member of the constituent assembly from 1990 to 1991.
achievements: Became a revered local Sufi saint
His tomb became a shrine in Bani Hafiz
Raised his orphaned nephew who became a prominent politician

figures.name: Sahibzada Irfan Danish
figures.title: Member of Constituent Assembly
figures.roleDescription: Political representative in Azad Kashmir government
figures.significance: Served as member of constituent assembly from 1990 to 1991, continuing the family's political involvement.
figures.biography: Sahibzada Irfan Danish was the son of Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus, the prominent Sufi saint of Bani Hafiz. He followed in the family tradition of public service by entering politics and serving as a member of the constituent assembly from 1990 to 1991. He was the cousin of Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar, who also served as a member of the legislative assembly during the same period (1990-1991), showing that both cousins were active in Azad Kashmir politics simultaneously. His political service represented the continued involvement of the Bani Hafiz family in the governance and political affairs of the region.
achievements: Member of constituent assembly of Azad Kashmir (1990-1991)

figures.roleDescription: Principal of degree college and educator

figures.biography: Anwar Kasur was the daughter of Hafiz Muhammad Yunus, the revered Sufi saint of Bani Hafiz. She dedicated her career to education and served as the principal of degree college for girls in Hattian Bala. Her contributions to education were so significant that the institute was later named after her, demonstrating the high regard in which she was held by the community. She was also noted as a poet of merit, showing her intellectual and literary capabilities beyond her educational administration role. She was the sister of politician Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar, indicating that the family produced notable figures in both education and politics.
achievements: Principal of degree college for girls Hattian Bala
Educational institute named in her honor

figures.biography: Hafiz Muhammad Shafi is the son of Hafiz Muhammad Qudous (also known as Molvi Bajee), making him part of the spiritual lineage of the Bani Hafiz family. He has become a famous saint of the Muzaffarabad region, continuing the family's long tradition of spiritual leadership that dates back to his ancestor Hafiz Jamal ud din. Many people come to him seeking spiritual guidance, indicating his significant influence and reputation as a spiritual leader in the region. He is described as being among the spiritual personalities of the vicinity and is a cousin of the late politician Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar. His prominence as a saint demonstrates the continued spiritual influence of the Bani Hafiz family in the region.
achievements: Became a famous saint of Muzaffarabad region

figures.name: Massimo Scali
figures.title: Italian Olympic ice dancing competitor and choreographer
figures.roleDescription: Olympic ice dancing competitor turned skating coach and choreographer
figures.significance: Former Italian Olympic ice dancer who became a prominent figure skating coach and choreographer, working with elite skaters including Alysa Liu.
figures.biography:
Massimo Scali is a former Italian Olympic ice dancing competitor who transitioned into coaching and choreography after his competitive career. He is now based in Oakland, California, where he works as a skating coach and choreographer. Scali began working with American figure skater Alysa Liu on her skating skills and choreography at the end of 2019, helping to develop her artistic presentation alongside her technical abilities.

Scali's coaching expertise extends beyond choreography to include skating skills development. He worked with Liu during her early competitive years, and after her retirement and subsequent comeback, she returned to work with both Scali and coach Phillip DiGuglielmo. Due to being based in the Bay Area while Liu trains in Southern California, Scali primarily works with her remotely, demonstrating his adaptability in modern coaching methods. His background as an Olympic competitor gives him unique insight into the pressures and requirements of elite-level figure skating.

Worked with multiple-time U.S. national champion Alysa Liu

figures.name: Muhammad of Ghor
figures.title: Ghurid Sultan
figures.roleDescription: Conqueror of northern India and co-ruler of the Ghurid Empire
figures.significance: Conquered northern India and established the foundations of Muslim rule in the Indian subcontinent.
figures.biography:
Muhammad of Ghor, also known as Shihabuddin Ghuri, was the younger brother of Ghiyath al-Din Muhammad and served as the junior partner in their dyarchy. While Ghiyath controlled the western territories from Fīrōzkōh, Muhammad was responsible for eastern expansion, particularly into the Indian subcontinent. In 1173, after multiple attempts, he reconquered Ghazni from the Ghuzz Turks. In 1175, he began his systematic conquest of northern India, initially crossing the Indus River through the Gomal Pass.

Muhammad's Indian campaigns were largely successful despite an early defeat in Gujarat at the Battle of Kasahrada in 1178. He conquered key territories including Multan, Sindh, and Peshawar, and eliminated the last Ghaznavid principality in Punjab by capturing Lahore in 1186. His most famous victories came against the Rajput confederacy led by Prithviraja III - after losing the First Battle of Tarain in 1191, he returned with Turkish mounted archers and won decisively in the Second Battle of Tarain in 1192. His generals, particularly Qutb ud-Din Aibak, conquered Delhi in 1193 and pushed eastward, defeating Jayachandra at the Battle of Chandawar in 1194. Muhammad was assassinated near Jhelum in March 1206 by Ismāʿīlīs, ending the unified Ghurid Empire.
achievements: Conquered northern India and established Muslim rule
Defeated the Rajput confederacy at the Second Battle of Tarain
Established the foundations of the Delhi Sultanate
Eliminated the last Ghaznavid stronghold in Lahore

figures.title: Ghurid King
figures.roleDescription: Known as 'Jahānsūz' (world burner) for sacking Ghazni, established Ghurid independence from Ghaznavids

figures.name: Qutb ud-Din Aibak
figures.title: General and later Sultan of Delhi
figures.roleDescription: Turkic general who conquered Delhi and became first Sultan of Delhi after Muhammad's assassination

figures.name: Shansab
figures.title: Founder
figures.roleDescription: Legendary founder of the Ghurid dynasty
figures.significance: Established the Ghurid dynasty according to traditional accounts.
figures.biography:
According to Minhaj-i-Siraj in Tabaqat-i-Nasiri, Shansab established the Ghurid dynasty. The account claims he was converted by the Arab Caliph Ali, though this is dismissed as unlikely by historians Mohammad Habib and K. A. Nizami. The early history of Ghor and the Ghurid origins remain unclear, with various legendary accounts providing different versions of the dynasty's founding.

The Ghurid family was known by the actual name Āl-e Šansab (Persianized: Šansabānī), which is the Arabic pronunciation of the originally Middle Persian name Wišnasp. The dynasty originated from the Ghor region in present-day central Afghanistan, where they initially started out as local chiefs before gradually converting to Sunni Islam and expanding their territorial control.

figures.name: Amir Banji
figures.title: Ghurid Prince
figures.roleDescription: Early Ghurid ruler whose rule was legitimized by the Abbasids
figures.significance: His rule was legitimized by the Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid, establishing Ghurid authority.
figures.biography:
Amir Banji was a certain Ghurid prince who was the ruler of Ghor and ancestor of the medieval Ghurid rulers. His rule was legitimized by the Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid, which provided important political validation for the Ghurid dynasty's authority in the region.

According to Minhaj-i-Siraj, the Ghurid Amir Faulad, who was presumably connected to this lineage, assisted Abu Muslim in overthrowing the Umayyads during the Abbasid Revolution. A legend recounts a dispute between two prominent families of the Ghor area, and they sought the intercession of the Abbasids, with the ancestor of the Shansabi family being subsequently confirmed as the ruler by Harun al-Rashid.
achievements: Legitimized Ghurid rule through Abbasid recognition

figures.name: Amir Suri
figures.title: King of the Ghurid dynasty
figures.roleDescription: 9th-10th century Ghurid ruler who fought against the Saffarids
figures.significance: Known for resisting the Saffarid conquest and maintaining Ghurid independence in the region.
figures.biography:
Amīr Sūrī was the king of the Ghurid dynasty from the 9th-century to the 10th-century. According to some legends, he was a descendant of the Ghurid king Amir Banji, whose rule was legitimized by the Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid. Amir Suri is known to have fought the Saffarid ruler Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar, who managed to conquer much of Khurasan except Ghur.

Amir Suri was later succeeded by his son Muhammad ibn Suri. Although Amir Suri bore an Arabic title and his son had an Islamic name, they followed Buddhism and were considered pagans by the surrounding Muslim people. It was only during the reign of Muhammad's son Abu Ali ibn Muhammad that the Ghurid dynasty became an Islamic dynasty. The Ghurids originated from the Ghuristan mountains, and were divided into numerous tribes, among which, the Shansabani tribe had the most authority.
achievements: Successfully resisted Saffarid conquest of Ghor
Maintained Ghurid independence against Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar

figures.name: Abu Ali ibn Muhammad
figures.title: Ghurid Ruler
figures.roleDescription: First Muslim ruler of the Ghurid dynasty
figures.significance: Converted the Ghurid dynasty to Islam, ending their Buddhist period.
figures.biography:
Abu Ali ibn Muhammad was the grandson of Amir Suri and son of Muhammad ibn Suri. He represents a crucial turning point in Ghurid history as the first ruler to convert the dynasty to Islam. Before his reign, the Ghurids followed Buddhism and were considered pagans by the surrounding Muslim people, despite bearing Arabic titles and Islamic names.

According to historical accounts, the early Ghurids followed Paganism before being converted to Islam by Abu Ali ibn Muhammad in 1149. His conversion marked the transformation of the Ghurid dynasty from a Buddhist principality to an Islamic one, which would later enable their expansion and integration into the broader Islamic world. Under his influence, Islam was firmly established among the Ghurid ruling class.
achievements: Converted the Ghurid dynasty to Islam
Established Islamic rule in Ghor

figures.name: Qutb al-Din Muhammad
figures.title: Ghurid Leader
figures.roleDescription: Local Ghurid leader who was poisoned by the Ghaznavids
figures.significance: His death sparked the Ghurid-Ghaznavid conflict that led to Ghurid independence.
figures.biography:
Qutb al-Din Muhammad was a local Ghurid leader in the mid-12th century during the period when the Ghurids were still bound to the Ghaznavids and Seljuks. In 1149, he had taken refuge in the city of Ghazni after having a quarrel with his brother Sayf al-Din Suri. However, the Ghaznavid ruler Bahram-Shah of Ghazna poisoned him while he was seeking sanctuary.

His death became the catalyst for the Ghurid war of independence. In revenge for his brother's murder, Sayf marched towards Ghazni and defeated Bahram-Shah, though Sayf was later captured and crucified at Pul-i Yak Taq when Bahram returned with reinforcements. This cycle of revenge ultimately led to Ala al-Din Husayn's devastating sack of Ghazni, earning him the title 'Jahānsūz' (the world burner), and marked the beginning of serious Ghurid resistance to Ghaznavid authority.

figures.name: Sayf al-Din Suri
figures.title: Ghurid Leader
figures.roleDescription: Brother of Qutb al-Din Muhammad who sought to avenge his death
figures.significance: Led the first major Ghurid military response against the Ghaznavids in revenge for his brother's murder.
figures.biography:
Sayf al-Din Suri was the brother of Qutb al-Din Muhammad and played a crucial role in the early Ghurid resistance against the Ghaznavids. When his brother was poisoned by the Ghaznavid ruler Bahram-Shah of Ghazna in 1149, Sayf immediately set out to seek revenge. He marched towards Ghazni and managed to defeat Bahram-Shah in the initial confrontation.

However, one year later in 1150, Bahram-Shah returned with reinforcements and scored a decisive victory against Sayf. Following his defeat, Sayf was captured and met a brutal end - he was crucified at Pul-i Yak Taq. His death, along with that of his brother, further fueled the cycle of revenge that would eventually lead to Ala al-Din Husayn's devastating retaliation against the Ghaznavids. The brothers' deaths became rallying points for Ghurid resistance and independence movements.
achievements: Initially defeated Bahram-Shah of Ghazna in revenge for his brother

figures.name: Hamdan ibn Hamdun al-Taghlibi

figures.name: Abdullah I Al-Sabah
figures.title: Second Ruler of Kuwait
figures.roleDescription: Second ruler of the Sheikhdom of Kuwait
figures.significance: Ruled Kuwait for 52 years from 1762 to 1814, consolidating the Al-Sabah dynasty's power.
figures.biography:
Abdullah I bin Sabah Al-Sabah (1740 – 3 May 1814) was the second ruler of the Sheikhdom of Kuwait, ruling from 1762 to 1814. He was the youngest son of Sabah bin Jaber, the founder of the Al-Sabah dynasty, and succeeded his father upon the latter's death in 1762.

Abdullah I ruled for over five decades, one of the longest reigns in Kuwaiti history. During his long reign, he consolidated the power and influence of the Al-Sabah family and helped establish Kuwait as a significant trading hub in the Persian Gulf region. He was succeeded by his son Jaber I Al-Sabah, continuing the hereditary succession that remains in place today.
achievements: Ruled Kuwait for 52 years
Consolidated Al-Sabah dynasty power
Established Kuwait as a trading hub

figures.name: Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah
figures.title: Tenth Ruler of Kuwait
figures.roleDescription: Tenth ruler who founded Kuwait's military and was father to multiple future emirs
figures.significance: Founded the military of Kuwait and was the father of four future emirs including Jaber, Sabah, Nawaf, and Mishal.
figures.biography:
Damat Sheikh Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah (1885 – 29 January 1950) was the tenth ruler of the Sheikhdom of Kuwait from 29 March 1921 until his death. Ahmad was the son of Jaber II Al-Sabah and succeeded his uncle Salem Al-Sabah in February 1921. He was the lead cavalry commander, founder of the military of Kuwait and the Directorate of Public Security Force.

During his reign, Ahmad faced various challenges including requests for financial aid from Palestinian authorities in 1936, which he refused due to treaty relations with Britain. He served as minister of finance from 1940 to 1950. Ahmad was married several times and had many notable children who would later play crucial roles in Kuwait's history. His sons included four future emirs: Jaber Al-Ahmad (ruled 1977–2006), Sabah Al-Ahmad (ruled 2006–2020), Nawaf Al-Ahmad (ruled 2020–2023), and Mishal Al-Ahmad (current emir since 2023). Ahmad died in 1950 at Dasman Palace in Kuwait.
achievements: Founded the military of Kuwait
Established the Directorate of Public Security Force
Father of four future emirs of Kuwait

figures.name: Ahmad Al-Abdullah Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah
figures.title: Current Prime Minister of Kuwait
figures.roleDescription: Current Prime Minister and former oil minister and central banker
figures.significance: Current Prime Minister of Kuwait since 2024, with extensive experience in finance and oil sectors.
figures.biography:
Ahmad Al-Abdullah Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah (born 5 September 1952) is a Kuwaiti economist, politician and senior member of the ruling family who currently serves as Prime Minister of Kuwait since 15 May 2024. He received a bachelor's degree in finance from the University of Illinois in 1975 and began his career at the Central Bank of Kuwait from 1978 to 1987.

Sabah then worked at private finance institutions from 1987 to 1999, serving as chairman of Burgan Bank SAK. He held various ministerial positions including minister of finance (1999-2001), minister of communication (1999), and oil minister (2009-2011). He was appointed Prime Minister-designate on 15 April 2024 and officially began his premiership on 15 May 2024. He is married with three children and was previously head of the Crown Prince's Court in 2021.
achievements: Current Prime Minister of Kuwait since 2024
Former Minister of Oil (2009-2011)
Former Chairman of Burgan Bank SAK

figures.name: Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah
figures.title: Early Jarrahid Leader
figures.roleDescription: First documented member of the Jarrahid family and ally of the Qarmatians
figures.significance: The first member of the Banu al-Jarrah to be mentioned in historical records and father of the powerful Mufarrij ibn Daghfal.
figures.biography:
Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah was the first member of the Banu al-Jarrah to appear in historical records and served as an important ally of the Qarmatians in the 10th century. He was based in al-Ramla, the center of Jund Filastin (District of Palestine), where he provided safe haven for an officer of the Qarmatian ruler Abu Tahir al-Jannabi when the latter departed to lead an expedition against Fatimid Egypt in 972 CE. Some sources suggest that Hassan ibn al-Jarrah, who was a commander of auxiliaries in the Qarmatian army during a second invasion of Egypt in 974, may have been the same person as Daghfal.

Daghfal's most significant contribution to his family's legacy was as the father of Mufarrij ibn Daghfal, who would become the most powerful Jarrahid chieftain and establish the family's dominance over Palestine. Through his alliance with the Qarmatians and his strategic position in al-Ramla, Daghfal laid the groundwork for his family's future political prominence in the region.
achievements: First documented member of the Jarrahid family in historical records
Provided safe haven for Qarmatian officers in al-Ramla
Established Jarrahid presence in Jund Filastin
Father of the powerful Mufarrij ibn Daghfal

figures.name: Hazim ibn Ali
figures.title: Jarrahid Chieftain
figures.roleDescription: Nephew of Hassan ibn Mufarrij and later Jarrahid leader
figures.significance: One of the later Jarrahid chieftains who attempted to wrest control of Damascus and became an ancestor of the Al Fadl dynasty.
figures.biography:
Hazim ibn Ali was a nephew of Hassan ibn Mufarrij and emerged as one of the later Jarrahid chieftains in the 11th century. In 1065/66, he and his cousin Humayd ibn Mahmud likely backed Abd al-Sharif ibn Abi'l Jann in his attempt to wrest control of Damascus from the troops of Fatimid vizier Badr al-Jamali. This political venture ended in failure, and both nephews were captured and imprisoned in Cairo. Their release was requested by the Fatimid general and descendant of the Hamdanids, Nasir al-Dawla ibn Hamdan, in 1066/67, suggesting they maintained some political importance even in captivity.

Hazim had sons named Badr and Rabi'a, with the latter becoming significant in the continuation of the Jarrahid lineage. Rabi'a served as an emir of Bedouin auxiliaries for the Burid ruler of Damascus, Toghtekin, who ruled from 1103 to 1128. Through Rabi'a's sons, particularly Fadl ibn Rabi'ah, Hazim became an ancestor of the Al Fadl dynasty, whose emirs would dominate the Bedouin of the steppe until the 18th century.
achievements: Attempted to wrest control of Damascus from Fatimid forces
Ancestor of Rabi'a and the Al Fadl dynasty
Maintained political significance even after imprisonment

figures.name: Humayd ibn Mahmud
figures.title: Jarrahid Chieftain
figures.roleDescription: Nephew of Hassan ibn Mufarrij and political ally of Hazim ibn Ali
figures.significance: Participated in the attempt to control Damascus alongside Hazim ibn Ali and represented the later generation of Jarrahid leadership.
figures.biography:
Humayd ibn Mahmud was another nephew of Hassan ibn Mufarrij who played a role in the later political activities of the Jarrahid family. Like his cousin Hazim ibn Ali, he was involved in the attempt to wrest control of Damascus from the Fatimid forces in 1065/66, supporting Abd al-Sharif ibn Abi'l Jann in this endeavor. The venture proved unsuccessful, resulting in both Humayd and Hazim being captured and imprisoned in Cairo by the troops of Fatimid vizier Badr al-Jamali.

Their imprisonment demonstrated the continued political ambitions of the Jarrahids even after the peak of their power under Mufarrij and Hassan. The fact that their release was specifically requested by Nasir al-Dawla ibn Hamdan, a prominent Fatimid general, in 1066/67 suggests that they retained some political value and connections even in captivity. Humayd represented the later generation of Jarrahid leaders who attempted to maintain their family's political influence in the changing landscape of 11th-century Syria and Palestine.
achievements: Participated in attempt to control Damascus alongside Hazim ibn Ali
Maintained political connections with prominent Fatimid officials

figures.title: Theatre Director
figures.roleDescription: Director of Armenian theatre and mother of filmmaker Rouben Mamoulian
figures.significance: She served as director of the Armenian theatre and was from a family of wealthy landowners and financiers.
figures.biography:
Virginie Kalantarian Mamoulian (Armenian: կոյս Քալանթարեան Մամուլեան) was born into a family of wealthy landowners and financiers. She served as a director of the Armenian theatre, demonstrating the cultural prominence of the Kalantarian family in Armenian artistic circles. She was married to Zachary Mamoulian, a bank president, and together they raised their son Rouben and daughter Svetlana in the Armenian Apostolic faith in Tiflis, Russian Empire.

The family was sympathetic to the Czarist regime and fled Russia during the turmoil of the 1917 revolution and the ensuing civil war. Her cultural background and involvement in Armenian theatre likely influenced her son Rouben's later career as a prominent film and theater director. The Kalantarian family's wealth and cultural engagement positioned them as notable figures in Armenian society of their time.
achievements: Served as director of Armenian theatre

figures.name: Petros Kalantarian
figures.title: Physician
figures.roleDescription: Notable physician from New Julfa
figures.significance: He was a prominent physician in the New Julfa Armenian community during the 18th century.
figures.biography:
Petros Kalantarian was born in 1735 in New Julfa, the Armenian quarter of Isfahan, Iran. He was part of the established Armenian community that had been relocated there by Shah Abbas the Great in the early 17th century. As a physician, Kalantarian represented the educated professional class that emerged within the New Julfa community.

The Kalantarian family was among the notable families of New Julfa, which had become a major hub of Armenian trade networks spanning from Amsterdam to Manila. The Armenian community of New Julfa was known for producing merchants, artisans, and professionals who played important roles in international commerce and cultural preservation. Petros Kalantarian's profession as a physician demonstrates the family's involvement in learned professions beyond commerce.
achievements: Practiced as physician in New Julfa

figures.name: Khedouri Zilkha
figures.title: Iraqi Banker
figures.roleDescription: Founder and patriarch of the Zilkha banking dynasty
figures.significance: Founded the Zilkha bank in 1899, establishing one of the Middle East's prominent banking dynasties.
figures.biography:
Khedouri Zilkha (1884–1956) was an Iraqi banker who established the foundation of what would become one of the Middle East's most significant banking dynasties. In 1899, he founded the Zilkha bank, which would grow to become a major financial institution in the region. The bank operated successfully across multiple Middle Eastern countries for several decades.

However, the family faced severe challenges during the mid-20th century when their banking operations were nationalized by governments in Iraq, Syria, and Egypt between 1950 and 1956. This forced the family to rebuild their financial empire in the United States and Europe. Despite these setbacks, Khedouri Zilkha's legacy lived on through his descendants, who successfully reestablished the family's prominence in international banking and finance, particularly in the United States.
achievements: Founded the Zilkha bank in 1899
Established a major Middle Eastern banking dynasty
Built successful banking operations across multiple countries

figures.name: Rose Tarlow
figures.title: Interior Designer
figures.roleDescription: Interior designer, furniture and textile designer, and author
figures.significance: Renowned interior designer known for creating elegant residences for notable clients and author of design books.
figures.biography:
Rose Tarlow (née Khedouri) is an interior designer, furniture and textile designer, and author based in Los Angeles, California. She graduated from Emerson College in 1960 with a Bachelor of Science in Theater Arts and married in 1961. After attending classes at the New York School of Interior Design and the Parsons School of Design, she established an interior design shop in Englewood, New Jersey. In 1971, having divorced and moved to California, she married lawyer Barry Tarlow.

She established Rose K. Tarlow Antiques. Ltd. in 1974, and Rose Tarlow Melrose House in 1981. Tarlow is known for creating rooms with highly refined wood, plaster, and stone finishes, furnished with antiques, and infused with a personal blend of minimalism and romanticism. Her notable projects include residences for Barbara Walters, David Geffen, and the Eli and Edythe Broad residence designed with Frank Gehry. She has authored books including 'The Private House' (2001) and 'Three Houses' (2022).
achievements: Established successful interior design businesses
Designed notable residences for prominent clients including Barbara Walters and David Geffen
Author of acclaimed design books

figures.name: Ezra Zilkha
figures.title: American Financier
figures.roleDescription: Prominent banker and financier from the Zilkha banking family
figures.significance: Member of the prominent Zilkha banking family that rebuilt their financial empire after nationalization in the Middle East.
figures.biography:
Ezra Zilkha (1925–2019) was an American financier and member of the prominent Zilkha banking family. The Zilkha family were bankers from the Middle East, with the patriarch Khedouri Zilkha establishing their bank in 1899. The family faced significant challenges when their assets were nationalized in three countries from 1950 to 1956 (Iraq, Syria and Egypt), forcing them to rebuild their financial empire in the US and Europe.

Ezra Zilkha was the father of Donald Zilkha, who became a successful American financier and businessman. The family's resilience and ability to rebuild their banking operations after losing their Middle Eastern assets demonstrates their business acumen and adaptability. Ezra's legacy continued through his son's successful career in finance and investment.
achievements: Rebuilt family banking operations after Middle Eastern nationalizations
Established successful financial career in the United States

figures.name: Donald Zilkha
figures.title: American Financier and Businessman
figures.roleDescription: Founder of multiple investment companies and business ventures
figures.significance: Successful financier who rebuilt the family's business empire in America after Middle Eastern nationalizations.
figures.biography:
Donald Elias Zilkha was born on April 8, 1951, in New York as the son of Ezra Zilkha. The Zilkha family were bankers from the Middle East whose patriarch Khedouri Zilkha established their bank in 1899, but the family was nationalized in three countries from 1950 to 1956 (Iraq, Syria and Egypt) and had to rebuild in the US and Europe. Donald attended Lycee Francais de New York before attending The Hill School, then graduated from Wesleyan University's College of Social Studies in 1973.

Zilkha joined J.P. Morgan in 1973 and worked in positions of increasing responsibility in tax incentivized transactions, restructuring and mergers & acquisitions. In 1982, he joined James D. Wolfensohn & Company, and in 1987 formed his own investment advisory boutique, Zilkha & Company. By 1994, he acquired 95% of Colt's Manufacturing Company, the historic American firearms manufacturer, restructuring it over 19 years. He subsequently established Zilkha Venture Partners (1999) and Zilkha Partners L.P. (2008), becoming a trustee emeritus of Wesleyan University and member of The Council on Foreign Relations.
achievements: Founded Zilkha & Company, Zilkha Venture Partners, and Zilkha Partners
Successfully acquired and restructured Colt's Manufacturing Company
Became trustee emeritus of Wesleyan University

figures.name: Houda Ezra Ebrahim Nonoo
figures.title: Ambassador and Politician
figures.roleDescription: First Jewish ambassador from any Middle Eastern Arab country
figures.significance: First Jewish ambassador of any Middle Eastern Arab country, serving as Bahraini Ambassador to the United States from 2008 to 2013.
figures.biography:
Houda Ezra Ebrahim Nonoo was born on September 7, 1964, in Manama to a family of Jewish business entrepreneurs with origins in present-day Iraq. After her father died in a car accident, she returned to Bahrain to take over the Basma Company, becoming a successful businesswoman. She lived for an extended period in the United Kingdom, where she attended Carmel College, a Jewish boarding school, and earned an MBA. She married Salman Idafar, a British Jew, with whom she had two sons, Menasheh and Ezra.

In 2004, she founded and presided over the Bahrain Human Rights Watch Society, advocating for women's rights and foreign workers' rights in Bahrain. She was appointed to the Majlis al-shura in 2005 by King Hamad ibn Isa Al Khalifah, serving for three years as a member of the 40-member Shura Council. On July 3, 2008, she was appointed Ambassador of the Kingdom of Bahrain to the United States, becoming the first Jew and third woman to serve as Bahraini ambassador, and notably the first Jewish ambassador from any Middle Eastern Arab country. Her appointment was responsible for diplomatic representation to Canada, Mexico, Brazil, and Argentina as well. During her tenure, she introduced interfaith initiatives and changed embassy traditions to be more inclusive. Her role ended in November 2013 when she was replaced by Abdullah bin Mohammad bin Rashed Al Khalifa.
achievements: First Jewish ambassador from any Middle Eastern Arab country
Founded Bahrain Human Rights Watch Society
First Jewish member of Bahraini Shura Council
Introduced interfaith diplomatic initiatives

figures.name: Nancy Dinah Elly Khedouri
figures.title: Politician and Author
figures.roleDescription: Member of Bahraini National Assembly and historian of Bahraini Jewish community
figures.significance: Member of the National Assembly of Bahrain since 2010 and author documenting the history of Bahraini Jews.
figures.biography:
Nancy Dinah Elly Khedouri was born in 1975 into the Khedouri family, one of the Jewish families in Bahrain involved in importing tablecloths and linens. She is third generation in Bahrain and is the cousin of Houda Nonoo, the former Bahraini Ambassador to the United States. Her family represents one of the established Jewish merchant families that have been part of Bahrain's commercial and social fabric.

In 2007, she published 'From Our Beginning to Present Day' (Arabic: بداياتنا و حتى وقتنا الحاضر), documenting the history of Bahraini Jews from the first settlers in the late 1880s to present day. In 2010, she became a member of the National Assembly of Bahrain, replacing Houda Nonoo in parliament. She has worked on improving relationships with Israel, appearing publicly with political figures such as Yisrael Katz, and represented Bahrain at the World Jewish Congress in April 2017. Her book emphasized Bahrain's religious tolerance and the peaceful coexistence between Jewish and Muslim communities in the kingdom.
achievements: Author of 'From Our Beginning to Present Day'
Member of National Assembly of Bahrain since 2010
Represented Bahrain at World Jewish Congress
